title: POC scope & model shortlist — confirmation, eval checklist, next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@fintura.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 12 Sep 2028 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: POC scope & model shortlist — confirmation, eval checklist, next steps

Hi Emma — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps for the pilot so we can lock timelines this week.

Summary of goals (what Fintura prioritized):
- Improve customer message classification and routing for English + Spanish (priority) and Portuguese (nice-to-have).
- Reduce manual triage by ~60% for top 5 intent categories.
- Meet latency SLOs for interactive workflows (p95 target ~150–200ms).

Proposed POC scope (2-week technical pilot):
1) Ingest: sample dataset (up to 10k messages) — you mentioned export access to anonymized support messages.
2) Models to evaluate (open models only):
   - Primary candidate: Mistral-7B-Instruct (baseline for quality/cost balance)
   - Higher-capacity candidate: Llama-3-13B-QLoRA (for tougher intent disambiguation)
   - Fallback / lightweight: Llama-2-7B (for cost-sensitive routing and as a graceful fallback)
3) Multilingual strategy: evaluate English + Spanish together; run a separate Portuguese slice if sample volume allows.
4) Evaluation: run automated intent classification tests + manual spot-checks for hallucination. See proposed acceptance metrics below.

Acceptance / success criteria (draft):
- Intent classification macro-F1 >= 0.85 on English test set
- Spanish intent accuracy >= 0.80 (or F1 >= 0.80)
- p95 response latency <= 200ms under expected batch profile
- Fallback invocation rate < 2% (i.e., model returns "low-confidence" and triggers simpler flow)
- No security/red-flag data leakage in spot checks

Operational constraints & asks from Fintura:
- We need a 24-hour access window to a tokenized, anonymized sample dataset.
- Please complete the attached security questionnaire for our legal/hosting guidance.

Owners (proposed):
- Sales (POC owner): Ben Carter (me) — overall project coordination, SOW/contract
- Technical lead: Vivek Kulkarni — model setup, quantization, perf tuning
- Evaluation & QA: Marissa Cole — test harness, manual spot checks, metric dashboard
- Customer success & rollout: Priyom Das — post-pilot acceptance and handoff

Deliverables & timeline (target start the week of Sep 18):
- Day 0: mutual NDA/SOW sign (if needed) and dataset delivery
- Days 1–3: model onboarding and baseline runs
- Days 4–9: evaluation runs + metric collection + manual QA
- Days 10–14: tuning + final acceptance report + recommendations (model for production, quantization profile, fallback policy)

Attachments: POC-scope-v1.pdf (detailed activities), security-questionnaire.xlsx

Next steps / ask for Fintura:
1) Confirm dataset availability and a point-of-contact for the export
2) Confirm Spanish support priority and whether Portuguese sample will be provided
3) Confirm we can schedule a 30-min kickoff Tuesday Sep 19 at 10:00am PT (or propose another time)

I'll loop in Priyom once we have a confirmed kickoff so he can align CS acceptance criteria.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: POC-scope-v1.pdf (22 KB), security-questionnaire.xlsx (34 KB)

-----
From: Emma Jones <emma.jones@fintura.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@fintura.com>
Date: Tue, 12 Sep 2028 16:18:00 -0400
Subject: Re: POC scope & model shortlist — confirmation, eval checklist, next steps

Thanks Ben — that summary looks good and captures the priorities. A few clarifications from our side:
- Spanish is our highest priority after English (we get ~30% Spanish volume in the support queue). Portuguese is lower priority but we can provide ~1k samples if helpful.
- Latency: 200ms p95 is acceptable for us for the pilot; if you can get to 150ms that's ideal but not required initially.
- Security: I attached our vendor questionnaire (Q&A) — some items will need our security team's review.

We can make a subset of anonymized messages available by Sep 16. Primary POC contact on our side is Liam O'Connor (cc'd).

I'll confirm kickoff time after checking Liam's calendar.

Best,
Emma
Director of Product, Fintura
emma.jones@fintura.com

Attachments: fintura_vendor_questionnaire.xlsx (attached)

-----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 13 Sep 2028 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: Fintura call recap + owners / ask

Internal — passing along the external confirmation from Emma. Key points for the team:
- Fintura will provide an anonymized export by Sep 16. Spanish: ~30% of volume; Portuguese: ~1k sample (optional).
- Pilot acceptance: Emma is OK with p95 <= 200ms for pilot. She reiterated classification quality priority.

Immediate asks:
- Vivek: plan for quantization & multi-model routing (how quickly can we spin Mistral-7B and Llama-3-13B-QLoRA runs in our hosted sandbox?). Also propose a fallback policy if the large model exceeds latency/cost thresholds.
- Marissa: draft an evaluation harness that captures macro-F1, per-intent recall, and human spot-check logs.
- Priyom: confirm CS acceptance checklist items & what handoff looks like post-pilot.

I've attached the POC-scope-v1.pdf in the external thread; forwarding here for reference.

Thanks — Ben

-----
From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 13 Sep 2028 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Internal: Fintura call recap + owners / ask

Quick technical notes:
- We can stand up Mistral-7B-Instruct in the sandbox within 24 hours once we have model weights and input format; Llama-3-13B-QLoRA will take longer (model conversion + qlora checkpoints) — estimate 3–4 days to run meaningful tests.
- Recommended quant profile for pilot: 8-bit PTQ for Mistral-7B; QLoRA for Llama-3-13B to retain higher quality with good memory footprint.
- Fallback policy suggestion: primary route to Llama-3-13B when confident score >= 0.7 AND expected latency < 200ms; otherwise route to Mistral-7B-Instruct; last resort Llama-2-7B for ultra-low-cost/very fast path.
- Perf targets: with continuous batching and KV cache tweaks, I think p95 160–200ms is achievable for single-request workloads for Mistral-7B; Llama-3-13B will likely be 220–320ms unless we accept 4-bit quant for speed (trade-off quality).

I'll prepare a short runbook for switching quant profiles and measuring resulting quality tradeoffs.

-Vivek
Senior SE, Redwood Inference

-----
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 14 Sep 2028 08:30:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Internal: Fintura call recap + owners / ask

Team — sketching an evaluation plan (I will formalize into a Google Doc):
- Test harness: we run 3 replicated experiments per model with fixed random seeds, measure macro-F1, per-intent precision/recall, false-positive rate for routing errors, and hallucination incidents (manual).
- Holdout splits: 70/15/15 (train/dev/test) if they provide labels; if unlabeled, we will label a 2k sample for testing.
- Manual QA: 100 random samples per language for spot-checks, captured in a shared spreadsheet.
- Post-pilot report: per-model recommendation with quantization profile, recommended production model, estimated cost per 1k tokens, and suggested fallback thresholds.

Acceptance thresholds (proposal to make explicit to customer and Priyom):
- Macro-F1 (English) >= 0.85
- Macro-F1 (Spanish) >= 0.80
- p95 latency <= 200ms (pilot)
- Hallucination rate (manual checks) < 3%

I'll create the doc and share the link by end of day; Vivek, can you confirm the expected test harness environment (GPU type + runtime) so we can include perf baselines?

Thanks,
Marissa
Solution Architect, Redwood Inference

-----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@fintura.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@fintura.com>
Date: Fri, 15 Sep 2028 11:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC kickoff proposed — please confirm dataset and time

Hi Emma / Liam — looping in the internal team. Thanks for confirming the sample delivery timeline. Quick checklist before kickoff: 
- Confirm dataset delivery (anonymized export) on Sep 16 — who will share?
- Confirm Spanish sample is included (yes per Emma) and Portuguese sample optional (we can ingest if provided).
- Please review the attached security questionnaire and flag any items that need special handling.

Proposed kickoff: Sep 19, 2028 at 10:00am PT — 30 minutes. Agenda: introductions, access & roles, immediate ops for model runs, and timeline check.

If that works, we'll send calendar invite and a link to the shared evaluation doc.

Thanks — looking forward.

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: POC-scope-v1.pdf, security-questionnaire.xlsx

title: AirWall Health — networking & residency qualification + action items
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Novak <elena.novak@airwallhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 12 Sep 2028 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: AirWall Health — networking & residency qualification + action items

Hi Elena — thanks again for the call earlier today. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth. Please flag anything missing or incorrect.

Summary of customer goals / pain points discussed:
- Strong regulatory driver: EU patient data must remain in-region and never egress to public internet (data residency + residency proof for audits).
- Air-gap requirement for production workload: inference must run in an isolated environment with zero outbound internet access except a one-way ingress for approved model artifacts (AirWall policy).
- Technical constraints: only private peering via dedicated cross-connect; no shared VPC endpoints; strict firewall rules that allow only specific IP ranges and TLS with mutual auth.
- Audit & key management: KMS/HSM-backed key control, support for customer-owned keys and per-request audit logs retained for 7 years.
- Performance & scale: expect ~30 RPS tail at 512 tokens with 95th percentile latency target <= 250ms for synchronous calls.

What Redwood can offer (high-level):
- Private deployment model (customer VPC or on-prem): we can run our private control plane with a closed-data-plane option and deliver a dedicated GPU pool within your VPC or on a physically isolated cluster if needed.
- Data residency controls: models and KV caches can be pinned to EU-resident storage and compute; no model checkpoints will leave the environment.
- Network posture options: support for an "airwall" pattern — inbound content bundle only (signed artifacts) and no outbound internet egress; or a VPC-peered deployment with strict routing.
- Security artifacts: SOC 2 report, encryption-at-rest details, and audit log schema — we will provide the evidence pack for legal and security review.

Open technical questions / clarifications we need from AirWall to scope a pilot and SOW:
1) Can you share a simple network diagram showing the intended cross-connect / colo provider and whether there is an existing private peering fabric? (attachment: airwall-network-diagram.pdf accepted)
2) Do you require a full air-gapped on-prem cluster or is a VPC-resident deployment in a dedicated customer account (no internet egress) acceptable?
3) Confirmation of KMS preference: customer-managed key (BYOK/HSM) vs Redwood-managed key for the pilot.
4) Expected pilot duration and success criteria (scale / latency targets, and a privacy checklist).
5) Procurement timeline and required commercial docs (NDA/POC SOW / security questionnaire).

Proposed next steps (timeline):
- Today/Tomorrow: I’ll upload our security summary + preliminary SOW draft and a short network requirements sheet.
- By Fri (2028-09-15): AirWall to share network diagram and procurement contact.
- Week of 2028-09-25: Technical deep-dive with infra + security (30–60m).
- Early Oct: Pilot kickoff (1–2 week validation window) if the connectivity and key-management model are agreed.

Attachments included in this email (stubs): airwall-network-diagram.pdf, airwall-security-requirements.xlsx

Action owners:
- Ben (me): coordinate security pack and draft SOW; deliver preliminary pilot baseline.
- Marissa: lead technical deep-dive scheduling and collect infra questions.
- Elena / AirWall: share network diagram and procurement contact (procurement@airwallhealth.com)

Thanks — happy to hop on a quick sync if helpful.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0129

---
References: short stubbed attachments included above.


From: Elena Novak <elena.novak@airwallhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tomas Rivera <tomas.rivera@airwallhealth.com>, Procurement <procurement@airwallhealth.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 13 Sep 2028 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AirWall Health — networking & residency qualification + action items

Thanks Ben — appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifications from our side:
- We do need proof that no outbound connections are required for inference once deployed. The only allowed inbound is the signed model bundle via our secure upload path.
- We prefer customer-managed keys (HSM-backed) for production. For the pilot, we can evaluate Redwood-managed keys if the SOW calls this out explicitly.
- Pilot success criteria: 95th-pctl latency <= 300ms at 25 RPS (the 30 RPS you called out is aspirational but acceptable as an upper bound).
- Procurement contact is procurement@airwallhealth.com — I’ve looped them. Expect a review of the security questionnaire and an initial PO template.

We’ll attach a simplified network diagram and list of allowed IP blocks by end of day Friday (2028-09-15). Also copying our CTO, Tomas, who will join the tech deep-dive.

Regards,
Elena Novak
Head of Integrations — AirWall Health
elena.novak@airwallhealth.com

Ben — thanks again for the demo this morning. Quick recap and the things we'd like Redwood to validate before we move forward:

We attached the sanitized requirements doc and a small seed set (1k prompts). We'd like an initial canary (500 prompts) within 5 business days and a full run in ~10 business days if possible. Also include estimated cost and any commercial constraints (usage limits, reserved capacity needs).

Team — forwarding Elena's request and the commitments we made. Need Rishi and Vivek to finalize the test harness and give time estimates. Key points to include in the test plan (drafted below):

Test plan (high level)
- Datasets: use Elena's supplied labeled subset (1k) and the 500-canary subset she approved. We'll also generate adversarial paraphrases (Rishi) to stress hallucination.
- Models/configs:
  * Redwood: redwood-3b-q (default), redwood-3b-fp16 (optional)
  * HyperscalerX-chat-v2 (baseline)
  * CloudGen-llm-3 (baseline)
- Metrics: hallucination_rate (defined as: asserted fact contradicting ground truth on factual prompts), semantic_accuracy, exact_match, p50/p95 latency, tokens_per_response, cost_per_1M_tokens. Set hallucination threshold for acceptance at <= 1.5% on the labeled factual subset.
- Regression checks: run 3 repeats with different RNG seeds; report mean + std. Highlight prompts with >10% variance in hallucination across seeds.
- Statistical significance: bootstrap 1k samples for confidence intervals on hallucination delta between Redwood and each hyperscaler.
- Output artifacts: raw outputs, token-level logs, small write-up (recommended config + examples).

Infra & scheduling
- Estimate compute: one dedicated GPU node (A100-class) for ~6 hours for full 1k x 3 repeats; canary ~45 minutes. Vivek — confirm resource needs and earliest slot.

Action items (owners)
- Rishi: finalize eval script, prepare eval-seeds.csv, and the hallucination adjudication rubric (due 2028-09-13).
- Vivek: reserve infra + run hyperscaler baseline connectivity checks (due 2028-09-14).
- Marissa: prepare pricing note for a one-off evaluation and any reserved-capacity pricing we should offer (due 2028-09-13).
- Ben: coordinate with Elena, schedule canary once infra & scripts ready.

Competitive positioning notes to include with the results (for the write-up)
- Measure latency with and without prefix/KV cache to show our caching win on repetitive prompts.
- Highlight dynamic routing to cheaper variants if latency SLOs can tolerate a slight quality delta.
- Include cost-per-token comparisons normalized for identical sequence lengths and batching.

Attachments: eval-seeds.csv (will contain the 500-canary list and the 1k labeled indices).

—Ben

title: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>, Priya Kapoor <priya.kapoor@axiombridge.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 10 Sep 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Hi Sanjay / Priya —

Thanks again for the call last week. We started a conservative sizing pass for the Dedicated slab you asked about and I attached our working model (ben_carter-capacity-model.xlsx). To make sure we align on the inputs before we lock an offer, can you confirm the operational profile below? If anything’s off, we’ll rejigger the curve and update the commitment math.

Quick summary of what we need from you:
- Steady-state TPS: avg tokens/sec across production day (not per-request tokens) — what should we assume?
- Campaign spikes: multiplier and typical duration (e.g., 4x for 20 minutes)
- P95 latency target you must meet on the Dedicated slab (ms)
- Any quiet-window schedule (night/weekends) we can use to downscale

Our preliminary assumptions (from the sample traces you shared):
- Baseline: 18,000 tokens/sec aggregate
- Peak burst (campaign): 96,000 tokens/sec (6x baseline for ~15–30m)
- Target P95 latency: 450ms for the top-level text-generation route

If those numbers look right we’ll produce:
1) a stress curve (steady -> peak -> tail) that maps to slab sizing,
2) an SLA draft and rollout plan that includes an autoscale corridor, and
3) a recommended reserve for KV-cache cold starts and model-quantization variance.

Attachment: ben_carter-capacity-model.xlsx (sheet: AXB_profile) — contains tokens/sec scenarios and GPU slab mapping.

Can you reply with the steady-state TPS and the typical campaign cadence? I’ll take that and produce a finalized GPU-fit proposal for your procurement team.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Lead, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0112

---
Redwood Inference — Developer-first LLM inference (hosted / dedicated / private)


From: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Kapoor <priya.kapoor@axiombridge.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Sep 2028 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Ben —

Thanks, this is helpful. Quick answers inline:

- Steady-state TPS: 21,500 tokens/sec (we measure tokens/sec across all multi-turn flows averaged over 1 minute windows)
- Campaign spikes: 5.5–6x (campaigns run 10–25 minutes, 2–3 campaigns/day on marketing days; usually single-day spikes during product launches)
- P95 latency: must be <= 500ms for the customer-facing /generate endpoint
- Quiet windows: nightly 02:00–05:00 PT we see ~25% of baseline; weekends ~40% of baseline

I’m attaching a sanitized extract of last month’s traffic for your model (axiombridge-production-metrics.csv). That has per-minute token counts and a few sample traces.

Question: how much cushion should we expect for KV-cache cold starts? We do a lot of multi-turn assistant work, so cold-cache behavior matters. Also procurement wants a clear line-item for any reserved GPUs vs burst/spot capacity.

Sanjay Reddy
Head of Product, AxiomBridge
Sanjay.Reddy@axiombridge.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>, Priya Kapoor <priya.kapoor@axiombridge.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Sep 2028 08:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Sanjay —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the CSV. I ran the file through our traffic synthesizer and produced a first-pass mapping. I attached an SLA draft for procurement (SLA-draft-axbridge.pdf) and updated the workbook. High-level mapping below — I kept math conservative to avoid under-provisioning:

Proposed Dedicated slab (baseline):
- Reserved: 5 x NVIDIA A100-80GB (fp16 path)
- Effective steady tokens/sec per GPU (observed): ~4,300 tps
- Aggregate baseline capacity (reserved): 21,500 tps (matches your steady-state)

Burst profile / autoscale:
- Autoscale up to 12 GPUs for sustained bursts (12 x 4,300 = 51,600 tps)
- We plan a short-term cold-burst lane using pre-warmed instances (2 extra on hot-standby) to absorb initial ramp for KV-cache cold misses

P95 latency fit (target <= 500ms):
- Baseline (5 GPUs): expected P95 ~360–420ms (fp16, warmed KV cache)
- At 6–8x bursts we rely on autoscale to keep latency under 500ms; if burst exceeds autoscale window we’ll serve degraded (fallback to smaller-model variant) rather than violate SLA

Reserve recommendation: +15% buffer on top of mapped reserve to account for:
- KV-cache miss variability
- Model quantization throughput variance
- Short-lived traffic fan-outs during campaign launches

Next steps I propose:
1) Confirm you’re OK with 5 reserved GPUs + autoscale to 12 (I’ll reflect pricing in the attached SLA).
2) Procurement review the SLA-draft and call out any billing/commit-period constraints.
3) Marcus to validate the per-GPU tps number against the exact model variant you’ll run (Mar: any red flags?).

I’ve linked the HubSpot deal and a DocuSign draft in the metadata. If that direction looks good I’ll push a pricing sheet and a firm commitment template.

Attachment: SLA-draft-axbridge.pdf

Best —
Ben


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Sep 2028 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Ben / Marcus — quick ops note:

I support the 5 reserved + autoscale approach, but please include an operational buffer of 15% on reserved GPU count in the procurement language. For AxiomBridge's multi-turn traffic the KV-cache cold miss can add non-trivial tail load during the first 10–20 minutes of a campaign.

Also flagging that we should include a two-week warmup period in the contract (reduced commit or ramp pricing) so SRE can pre-warm KV caches and observe real traffic before the full commit is enforced.

Kimberly Park
Head of Enterprise Ops, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Sep 2028 10:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

All — I validated the per-GPU throughput numbers against the model variants AxiomBridge mentioned on the call. Notes below:

Model assumptions:
- Primary model: LM-34B (FP16 / q4_0 path in our runtime) — this is the model we recommend for your quality/latency tradeoff.
- Fallback model: LM-7B-quant (int8-ish path) for deep-burst graceful degradation.

Measured throughput (typical prompt mix from your CSV):
- LM-34B @ A100-80GB (fp16 warmed): 4,100–4,400 tokens/sec per GPU (1-min windows) — use 4,300 for planning.
- LM-7B-quant @ A100-80GB (int8 warm): ~14,000 tokens/sec per GPU (used for fallback path)

KV-cache cold miss impact:
- Cold miss multiplier: ~1.6x CPU/GPU work for the first 30–90s of a session when large context loads; we bake this into the 15% buffer Kimberly requested.

Autoscale timing and throttles:
- Autoscale target: add one GPU per ~40k tokens/sec above baseline, with a 60s scale window to avoid flapping.
- For the 5 -> 12 surge we recommend a short pre-warm (2 GPUs) driven by a traffic trigger to handle the first 90s of a campaign burst.

Risk callouts:
- If AxiomBridge expects sustained 6x bursts longer than 30 minutes we should convert burst capacity to part of the reserved slab or agree fixed burst credits (cost tradeoff).
- Quantization variance: performance of LM-34B in q4 path can vary ±8% depending on prompt shape — keep a small cushion.

I’ll update Ben’s workbook with these validated per-GPU rates and a stress-curve graph so procurement can see the sizing line-items.

—Marcus
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

From: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Kapoor <priya.kapoor@axiombridge.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Sep 2028 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Ben —

Thanks, this is helpful. Quick answers inline:

- Steady-state TPS: 21,500 tokens/sec (we measure tokens/sec across all multi-turn flows averaged over 1 minute windows)
- Campaign spikes: 5.5–6x (campaigns run 10–25 minutes, 2–3 campaigns/day on marketing days; usually single-day spikes during product launches)
- P95 latency: must be <= 500ms for the customer-facing /generate endpoint
- Quiet windows: nightly 02:00–05:00 PT we see ~25% of baseline; weekends ~40% of baseline

I’m attaching a sanitized extract of last month’s traffic for your model (axiombridge-production-metrics.csv). That has per-minute token counts and a few sample traces.

Question: how much cushion should we expect for KV-cache cold starts? We do a lot of multi-turn assistant work, so cold-cache behavior matters. Also procurement wants a clear line-item for any reserved GPUs vs burst/spot capacity.

Sanjay Reddy
Head of Product, AxiomBridge
Sanjay.Reddy@axiombridge.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>, Priya Kapoor <priya.kapoor@axiombridge.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Sep 2028 08:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Sanjay —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the CSV. I ran the file through our traffic synthesizer and produced a first-pass mapping. I attached an SLA draft for procurement (SLA-draft-axbridge.pdf) and updated the workbook. High-level mapping below — I kept math conservative to avoid under-provisioning:

Proposed Dedicated slab (baseline):
- Reserved: 5 x NVIDIA A100-80GB (fp16 path)
- Effective steady tokens/sec per GPU (observed): ~4,300 tps
- Aggregate baseline capacity (reserved): 21,500 tps (matches your steady-state)

Burst profile / autoscale:
- Autoscale up to 12 GPUs for sustained bursts (12 x 4,300 = 51,600 tps)
- We plan a short-term cold-burst lane using pre-warmed instances (2 extra on hot-standby) to absorb initial ramp for KV-cache cold misses

P95 latency fit (target <= 500ms):
- Baseline (5 GPUs): expected P95 ~360–420ms (fp16, warmed KV cache)
- At 6–8x bursts we rely on autoscale to keep latency under 500ms; if burst exceeds autoscale window we’ll serve degraded (fallback to smaller-model variant) rather than violate SLA

Reserve recommendation: +15% buffer on top of mapped reserve to account for:
- KV-cache miss variability
- Model quantization throughput variance
- Short-lived traffic fan-outs during campaign launches

Next steps I propose:
1) Confirm you’re OK with 5 reserved GPUs + autoscale to 12 (I’ll reflect pricing in the attached SLA).
2) Procurement review the SLA-draft and call out any billing/commit-period constraints.
3) Marcus to validate the per-GPU tps number against the exact model variant you’ll run (Mar: any red flags?).

I’ve linked the HubSpot deal and a DocuSign draft in the metadata. If that direction looks good I’ll push a pricing sheet and a firm commitment template.

Attachment: SLA-draft-axbridge.pdf

Best —
Ben


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Sep 2028 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

Ben / Marcus — quick ops note:

I support the 5 reserved + autoscale approach, but please include an operational buffer of 15% on reserved GPU count in the procurement language. For AxiomBridge's multi-turn traffic the KV-cache cold miss can add non-trivial tail load during the first 10–20 minutes of a campaign.

Also flagging that we should include a two-week warmup period in the contract (reduced commit or ramp pricing) so SRE can pre-warm KV caches and observe real traffic before the full commit is enforced.

Kimberly Park
Head of Enterprise Ops, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Sanjay Reddy <sanjay@axiombridge.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Sep 2028 10:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AxiomBridge commit — capacity stress curve & GPU-fit plan

All — I validated the per-GPU throughput numbers against the model variants AxiomBridge mentioned on the call. Notes below:

Model assumptions:
- Primary model: LM-34B (FP16 / q4_0 path in our runtime) — this is the model we recommend for your quality/latency tradeoff.
- Fallback model: LM-7B-quant (int8-ish path) for deep-burst graceful degradation.

Measured throughput (typical prompt mix from your CSV):
- LM-34B @ A100-80GB (fp16 warmed): 4,100–4,400 tokens/sec per GPU (1-min windows) — use 4,300 for planning.
- LM-7B-quant @ A100-80GB (int8 warm): ~14,000 tokens/sec per GPU (used for fallback path)

KV-cache cold miss impact:
- Cold miss multiplier: ~1.6x CPU/GPU work for the first 30–90s of a session when large context loads; we bake this into the 15% buffer Kimberly requested.

Autoscale timing and throttles:
- Autoscale target: add one GPU per ~40k tokens/sec above baseline, with a 60s scale window to avoid flapping.
- For the 5 -> 12 surge we recommend a short pre-warm (2 GPUs) driven by a traffic trigger to handle the first 90s of a campaign burst.

Risk callouts:
- If AxiomBridge expects sustained 6x bursts longer than 30 minutes we should convert burst capacity to part of the reserved slab or agree fixed burst credits (cost tradeoff).
- Quantization variance: performance of LM-34B in q4 path can vary ±8% depending on prompt shape — keep a small cushion.

I’ll update Ben’s workbook with these validated per-GPU rates and a stress-curve graph so procurement can see the sizing line-items.

—Marcus
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

title: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty
preview:
From: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>; Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Cc: Evan Torres <evan.torres@novaforge.com>
Date: Fri, 25 Aug 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty

Hi Ben / Kimberly,

Thanks for the MSA draft. Our legal team reviewed the IP indemnity sections and came back with two asks that are blocking procurement sign-off:

1) Notice routing: we need clarity that "customer notice" (third-party IP claim) can be delivered to either party but that the counterparty has 30 days to acknowledge and confirm who will lead defense. Right now the draft implies all notices must go to Customer contact only. We need a dual-notice path.

2) Remediation warranty: NovaForge is asking for an explicit remediation warranty tied to infringement claims when compatibility or configuration provided by Vendor (you) caused the issue — we proposed a short warranty and a capped remediation fund.

Attached is a redline of the MSA (MSA-redline_v3.pdf) showing our suggested insertions under Section 7 and Section 9. We prefer a simple compromise so procurement can get to signature this week.

Proposed quick asks for discussion:
- Can Redwood accept a dual-notice routing with 30-day acknowledgement?
- Can Redwood add a remediation warranty limited to fixes/patches attributable to Redwood technical deliverables, capped at $250k per claim?

If you want, Evan (cc'd) and I can hop on a 20-min call tomorrow to walk through the specifics.

Thanks,
Sarah Li | Senior Counsel, NovaForge
E: sarah.li@novaforge.com | M: +1 415-555-0198

Attachments: MSA-redline_v3.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>; Evan Torres <evan.torres@novaforge.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 25 Aug 2028 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty

Sarah / Evan — thanks for the clear note and the annotated MSA. Looping in Kimberly (legal) and Marcus (contracts) to evaluate the two asks. A couple of quick commercial points from Sales so everyone has context:

- Dual-notice with an internal 30‑day ack window is reasonable operationally, and it helps customers feel they have a direct path. Our standard playbook is to require immediate notice to Redwood legal and SalesOps so we can triage. We can accept dual-notice if the acknowledgment/lead selection language is tight and we retain right to defend where appropriate.

- Remediation warranty: we need to limit scope to Redwood deliverables (not customer modifications or third-party integrations) and keep a per-claim cap. $250k is a starting point — legal will advise. Also would prefer indemnity to remain the primary risk-transfer tool (i.e., defend & indemnify for third-party claims) with remediation warranty as a narrow operational fallback.

Kimberly / Marcus — can you review Sarah's redline and advise: 1) whether dual-notice with 30-day ack is acceptable as a clean edit; 2) proposed language for remediation warranty that narrows scope and caps liability (aim for defense-first, remediation-second structure).

If helpful, I can schedule a 20-min sync with NovaForge on Monday.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
b: +1 650-555-0124

> On Fri, Aug 25, 2028 at 09:12 Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com> wrote:
> > Thanks for the MSA draft. Our legal team reviewed the IP indemnity sections and came back with two asks...


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>, Evan Torres <evan.torres@novaforge.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Aug 2028 10:15:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty

Hi all — reviewed Sarah's redline and Ben's notes. Below is a recommended compromise that keeps our commercial posture but addresses NovaForge's operational concerns. I attached an evidence checklist we often provide for indemnity claims (evidence-checklist.xlsx).

Principles:
- Defense-first: Redwood will continue to offer defend-and-indemnify for third-party IP infringement claims where Redwood's deliverables are alleged to infringe.
- Dual-notice: Accept dual-notice (customer or vendor) with a 30-day acknowledgement window. If Redwood acknowledges and elects to defend, we will control defense subject to standard cooperation provisions. If Redwood does not acknowledge within 30 days, Customer may assume defense and Redwood will reimburse reasonable costs up to the applicable indemnity limits.
- Remediation warranty narrow-scope: Offer a limited warranty to provide remedial code/patches or configuration fixes attributable to Redwood deliverables, capped at $250,000 per claim and $500,000 aggregate per 12-month period. This warranty excludes modifications by Customer, third-party components, and open-source repositories.

Proposed redline language (suggest inserting under new sub-section in Section 7):

"Notice and Defense Coordination. In the event Customer or Vendor receives a third-party claim alleging that the Services infringe a third party's intellectual property rights, the receiving party shall provide prompt written notice to the other party. Either party may deliver such notice. The receiving party shall acknowledge receipt within thirty (30) days and shall promptly confirm whether it will assume primary responsibility to defend the claim. If Vendor (Redwood) elects to defend, Vendor shall have the right to control the defense, subject to Customer's right to participate at its own expense. If Vendor declines or fails to assume defense within thirty (30) days, Customer may control the defense and Vendor shall reimburse Customer's reasonable defense costs up to the indemnity cap set forth in this Agreement."

"Remediation Warranty. Vendor warrants that, for a period of one (1) year following delivery of material Services, Vendor will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide remedial fixes, patches or configuration changes to cure any infringement arising from Vendor's deliverables, provided Customer promptly notifies Vendor and cooperates in good faith. Vendor's liability under this warranty is limited to $250,000 per claim and $500,000 in the aggregate per rolling twelve (12) month period. This warranty does not apply to (a) Customer modifications; (b) Customer-provided integrations or third-party components; or (c) open-source software claims where remedy is governed by the applicable OSS license."

These edits preserve our indemnity framework and give NovaForge the operational certainty they asked for. Marcus — if this looks good from an ops/insurance perspective, we can send as a single tracked-change response to NovaForge tomorrow.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Associate General Counsel | Redwood Inference
kimberly.park@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0101

Attachments: evidence-checklist.xlsx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>, Evan Torres <evan.torres@novaforge.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Aug 2028 16:38:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty — compromise proposal

Sarah / Evan — following Kimberly's suggested language (below), Marcus and I ran numbers/ops and we can accept the compromise as written: dual-notice with 30-day acknowledgement + the narrow remediation warranty (250k / 500k agg). The key conditions are (1) warranty limited to Redwood deliverables; (2) exclusion for customer modifications and third-party components; and (3) cooperation on evidence and triage per the attached checklist.

Next steps:
- We will upload a tracked-change MSA with Kimberly's exact text and return it to you by end of day tomorrow.
- If NovaForge confirms the language, procurement (Evan) and I will move the DocuSign envelope and target signature next week.
- Kimberly / Marcus: please confirm any final procurement/insurance hold points.

Sarah — can you confirm that the remediation cap figures are acceptable to NovaForge (250k per claim / 500k agg)? If yes, we'll mark the redline as "agreed in principle" and circulate for signature.

Thanks everyone — appreciate the efficient back-and-forth.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference

From: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>; Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>

Hi Ben / Kimberly,

Thanks for the MSA draft. Our legal team reviewed the IP indemnity sections and came back with two asks that are blocking procurement sign-off:

1) Notice routing: we need clarity that "customer notice" (third-party IP claim) can be delivered to either party but that the counterparty has 30 days to acknowledge and confirm who will lead defense. Right now the draft implies all notices must go to Customer contact only. We need a dual-notice path.

2) Remediation warranty: NovaForge is asking for an explicit remediation warranty tied to infringement claims when compatibility or configuration provided by Vendor (you) caused the issue — we proposed a short warranty and a capped remediation fund.

Attached is a redline of the MSA (MSA-redline_v3.pdf) showing our suggested insertions under Section 7 and Section 9. We prefer a simple compromise so procurement can get to signature this week.

Proposed quick asks for discussion:
- Can Redwood accept a dual-notice routing with 30-day acknowledgement?
- Can Redwood add a remediation warranty limited to fixes/patches attributable to Redwood technical deliverables, capped at $250k per claim?

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@novaforge.com>, Evan Torres <evan.torres@novaforge.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Aug 2028 10:15:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: MSA redline: IP infringement notice owner & remediation warranty

Hi all — reviewed Sarah's redline and Ben's notes. Below is a recommended compromise that keeps our commercial posture but addresses NovaForge's operational concerns. I attached an evidence checklist we often provide for indemnity claims (evidence-checklist.xlsx).

Principles:
- Defense-first: Redwood will continue to offer defend-and-indemnify for third-party IP infringement claims where Redwood's deliverables are alleged to infringe.
- Dual-notice: Accept dual-notice (customer or vendor) with a 30-day acknowledgement window. If Redwood acknowledges and elects to defend, we will control defense subject to standard cooperation provisions. If Redwood does not acknowledge within 30 days, Customer may assume defense and Redwood will reimburse reasonable costs up to the applicable indemnity limits.
- Remediation warranty narrow-scope: Offer a limited warranty to provide remedial code/patches or configuration fixes attributable to Redwood deliverables, capped at $250,000 per claim and $500,000 aggregate per 12-month period. This warranty excludes modifications by Customer, third-party components, and open-source repositories.

Proposed redline language (suggest inserting under new sub-section in Section 7):

"Notice and Defense Coordination. In the event Customer or Vendor receives a third-party claim alleging that the Services infringe a third party's intellectual property rights, the receiving party shall provide prompt written notice to the other party. Either party may deliver such notice. The receiving party shall acknowledge receipt within thirty (30) days and shall promptly confirm whether it will assume primary responsibility to defend the claim. If Vendor (Redwood) elects to defend, Vendor shall have the right to control the defense, subject to Customer's right to participate at its own expense. If Vendor declines or fails to assume defense within thirty (30) days, Customer may control the defense and Vendor shall reimburse Customer's reasonable defense costs up to the indemnity cap set forth in this Agreement."

"Remediation Warranty. Vendor warrants that, for a period of one (1) year following delivery of material Services, Vendor will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide remedial fixes, patches or configuration changes to cure any infringement arising from Vendor's deliverables, provided Customer promptly notifies Vendor and cooperates in good faith. Vendor's liability under this warranty is limited to $250,000 per claim and $500,000 in the aggregate per rolling twelve (12) month period. This warranty does not apply to (a) Customer modifications; (b) Customer-provided integrations or third-party components; or (c) open-source software claims where remedy is governed by the applicable OSS license."

These edits preserve our indemnity framework and give NovaForge the operational certainty they asked for. Marcus — if this looks good from an ops/insurance perspective, we can send as a single tracked-change response to NovaForge tomorrow.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Associate General Counsel | Redwood Inference
kimberly.park@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0101

Attachments: evidence-checklist.xlsx

title: HIPAA scope vs infrastructure fee — scope and SLA breakdown requested
preview:
From: Samantha Choe <samantha.choe@mediwell.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Mediwell Procurement <procurement@mediwell.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Aug 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: HIPAA scope vs infra fee — can you break this out?

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the platform looked good for our use cases. Procurement asked me to surface a couple of follow-ups before we move to the commercial review:

1) The quote we received seems to aggregate a "compliance/infrastructure" line. Can you provide a clean breakdown between the HIPAA-specific scope (audit logs + BAA obligations + attestation support) and the infrastructure/residency/KMS fees (HSM, dedicated residency, egress controls)? We need line items for internal cost modeling.

2) We also need to understand the SLA differences if we choose US-only residency vs hybrid US/EU for a subset of patient data. Do the HSM seat prices or audit-log retention costs change by region?

Attached is our standard procurement questionnaire (procurement_RFP_v1.pdf) — there are a few items that map directly to the pricing line items.

Could you send a consolidated worksheet and indicate any one-time implementation fees vs recurring? We have a pricing review on Thursday.

Best,
Samantha
Senior Sourcing Manager
Mediwell Health

Attachment: procurement_RFP_v1.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Choe <samantha.choe@mediwell.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 21 Aug 2028 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: HIPAA scope vs infra fee — can you break this out?

Hi Samantha — thanks for the clear ask and the RFP.

Short answer: yes, we can provide a line-by-line breakdown. I’ve drafted a worksheet that separates: (A) HIPAA governance/support scope (BAA, audit log ingestion & retention, SOC2 evidence packaging) — priced as a recurring add-on with an implementation ramp; (B) Cryptographic key management & HSM seat licensing — can be licensed per-seat or amortized as a monthly bundle; (C) Residency carve-outs (US vs EU) — residency surcharge by region and an optional data replication charge for geo-redundant configs.

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Choe <samantha.choe@mediwell.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 22 Aug 2028 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: HIPAA scope vs infra fee — can you break this out?

Samantha — thanks for the questions. Action plan and answers below.

1) Backfill estimate: we can provide a provisional backfill rate of $0.12/GB for bulk historical ingestion (one-time), with an accuracy band of ±20% until we see the dataset profile. I put this as a separate line in the attached single-sheet quote.

2) HSM seat amortization: two options — (A) Upfront: $45k per seat paid up front; (B) Amortized: $1,350/month over 36 months. If you convert later to Redwood-hosted KMS, we allow conversion with a 90-day notice; conversion fee = remaining amortized balance or a flat $6k conversion admin fee, whichever is lower. I captured both schedules in the matrix.

3) Mixed residency: supported via dataset-level routing. If you pin PHI datasets to US-only, non-PHI can be routed to EU; you only need HSM seats in the region where customer-managed keys will be active. So if you want two distinct customer-managed KMS instances (one in US, one in EU) you will need separate seats per region — we call this out as "per-region seats" in the quote.

Deliverables I will send by 10:30am PT tomorrow:
- Single-sheet consolidated quote (recurring vs one-time)
- SOW language draft for audit evidence delivery cadence (quarterly bundles)
- Example amortization schedule and DocuSign-ready term sheet

title: SLA measurement audit: evidence collection schedule & telemetry sample request
preview:
From: Jane Morris <jane.morris@sentinelbank.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@sentinelbank.com
Date: Fri, 10 Aug 2028 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RFP: SLA measurement — sample telemetry + evidence window

Ben —

Thanks for the demo last week. Procurement is preparing the final SOW and the technical team has one outstanding set of asks around how Redwood measures uptime/latency for the hosted inference API. To complete our internal risk review we need: 

1) A written description of the measurement pipeline you use to generate SLA metrics (what signals, sampling intervals, aggregation windows).

title: Re: Order Form + DPA: SCCs & role alignment (OptiMed)
preview:
From: Claire Novak <c.novak@optimed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: OptiMed Legal Ops <legal@optimed.com>
Date: Fri, 17 Aug 2028 13:05:00 +0100
Subject: MSA / Order Form redlines + SCCs (attached)

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the call earlier. Attached are two files: an annotated set of the SCCs we’d like to include and a marked-up DPA showing the few controller/processor role adjustments the product team requested. High-level asks:

- Can Redwood confirm whether it will accept our annotated SCCs as-is, or whether you require the standard EU Commission text with only our business-specific additions?
- We noted one area on sub-processor chaining and a short data export clause — would Redwood be able to accept the proposed language in the attached DPA markups, or do you need an explicit Transfer Impact Addendum?
- Timeline: we need a final MSA + Order Form packaged for signature by next week (target Wed 22 Aug).

Attachments: OptiMed_SCCs_annotated.pdf, OptiMed_DPA_markups.docx

Thanks — happy to jump on a short call if that helps.

Best,
Claire Novak
Head of Legal Ops
OptiMed Solutions

---


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Novak <c.novak@optimed.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 17 Aug 2028 16:31:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MSA / Order Form redlines + SCCs (attached)

Claire — thanks for sending and for the clear summary. Looping in Marissa (contracts) and Laura (privacy) to lead the redline review. Quick notes from product/legal ops (high level):

- Redwood’s default position is to act as a processor on customer data for the hosted inference product; we already sign standard SCCs in many EU deals but we do need to confirm a few of your custom annotations (particularly export controls / egress obligations).
- Sub-processor chaining: generally acceptable if we keep the standard flow-down clause and have a sub-processor list/exclusion path. If OptiMed needs explicit prior approval for a named category, we can accommodate with an exception in the Order Form, but that impacts timeline.
- Transfer Impact Addendum (TIA): if your markups introduce non-standard obligations (e.g., extra audit rights or specific routing), we’ll likely propose a short TIA addendum rather than reworking the full SCC text.

Marissa — can you take the attached markups and propose concrete language edits (MSA + Order Form + DPA) we can send back to OptiMed? If possible, include a short fallback that preserves their controls but keeps our sub-processor operational model. I’ve added the HubSpot deal for context: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/OPTI-2028-224

Claire — happy to schedule a 30-minute sync tomorrow early afternoon CET if that speeds alignment. Are you available 12:00–13:00 CET?

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Director, Business Development
Redwood Inference

---


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Claire Novak <c.novak@optimed.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Aug 2028 09:14:00 +0100
Subject: Re: MSA / Order Form redlines + SCCs (attached)

On Fri, 17 Aug 2028 at 16:31 Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Marissa — can you take the attached markups and propose concrete language edits (MSA + Order Form + DPA) we can send back to OptiMed?

Thanks Ben / Claire — I’ve reviewed the two documents. Attached are two items: a proposed DPA draft (Redwood_DPA_v3.docx) that incorporates OptiMed’s preferences where acceptable, and a short legal note (SCCs_Legal_Notes.pdf) that explains the choices. High-level summary and suggested redlines below — please consider these as our recommended starting point to preserve OptiMed’s controls while keeping operational clarity.

1) Processor / Controller language (MSA, §2):
Proposed snippet to add to MSA (order-form specific):
\"Redwood will process Personal Data on behalf of Customer in accordance with the Agreement and the DPA. For the avoidance of doubt, Redwood acts as Processor for all Customer Data processed via the Redwood hosted inference product, except to the extent Redwood has been expressly designated as Controller in a written order accepted by Redwood.\"

2) SCCs:
- We can accept customer-annotated SCCs where edits are clarifying (e.g., contact details, purpose). Where the SCC text deviates from the EC standard (new obligations or audit regimes), we propose keeping the EC text and adding a short Annex (Transfer Impact Addendum) to document the special controls/assurances.

3) Sub-processors and chaining (DPA, §4):
- Keep flow-down but add an Order Form checkbox that allows OptiMed to require prior notice for any sub-processor outside EU/EEA. That preserves their control without breaking our operational model. Proposed sentence: \"Customer may opt to require prior notice and approval for sub-processors located outside the EU/EEA by selecting the relevant option in the Order Form; absent such selection, Redwood may engage sub-processors following the notice described in §4.2.\"

4) Transfer Impact / hosting routing (TIA):
- Where OptiMed requires a specific routing or egress restriction, we will include a short TIA that describes technical safeguards (encryption at rest/in transit, limited roles with KMS, logging retention). We will not re-author the SCCs themselves but will reference the SCC implementation controls in the TIA Annex.

5) Audit / access requests:
- Customer audit rights: propose a controlled approach — allow self-certified questionnaires and periodic SOC2-type evidence; on-site audits only by exception and with agreed scope and cost allocation.

Next steps I propose:
- Redwood sends a tracked redline of the DPA + a redline of the MSA clause and a one-page TIA template. (Attached: Redwood_DPA_v3.docx)
- If OptiMed confirms the Order Form hosting selection (EU-only vs. multi-region), we can finalize the Order Form checkbox language and produce a final package for signature.

Attachments: Redwood_DPA_v3.docx, SCCs_Legal_Notes.pdf

Action: Claire, can you confirm whether OptiMed will accept an Order Form checkbox for \"EU-only hosting and sub-processor notice\" (this avoids a custom approval for every sub-processor). If yes, I will insert the exact language and share a DocuSign-ready package.

Marissa Cole
Senior Counsel, Contracts
Redwood Inference

---


From: Claire Novak <c.novak@optimed.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: OptiMed Legal Ops <legal@optimed.com>
Date: Tue, 19 Aug 2028 16:02:00 +0100
Subject: Re: MSA / Order Form redlines + SCCs (attached)

Marissa / Ben — thanks for the careful read and the proposed language. OptiMed is comfortable with the approach: accept the EC SCC text with an attached Transfer Impact Addendum describing controls, and the Order Form checkbox for \"EU-only hosting and sub-processor notice\" works for us.

Next steps from our side:
- We’ll accept Redwood_DPA_v3.docx subject to the small edits you flagged in SCCs_Legal_Notes.pdf.
- Please generate a DocuSign package with the MSA + Order Form + DPA + TIA Annex so we can review the final layout before signature. If you can get that to us by EOD Monday (21 Aug) we can route internally and aim for signatures during the week.

Thanks again — appreciate the pragmatic trade-offs.

Best regards,
Claire Novak
Head of Legal Ops
OptiMed Solutions

title: POC evaluation & implementation plan — next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@payline.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 11 Aug 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC evaluation & implementation plan — next steps

Hi Sophie — thanks again for taking the call this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment before we lock dates with your team. TL;DR: proposed kickoff 2028-08-21, 4-week trial window, engineering sync week of 8/21, success criteria + API contract attached.

What we covered (summary):
- Goal: validate Payline’s fraud-detection assistant using Redwood’s low-latency streaming + tool-calling for multi-step lookups.
- Success criteria (draft attached): throughput target 15 req/s sustained, P99 latency < 220ms for short requests, precision/recall targets on labelled evaluation set (we requested a 5k sample).
- Integration surface: streaming inference for real-time events, SDK route for batched re-rank jobs, and a tool-calling integration to hit internal transaction lookups.
- Data handling: you flagged PII residency and deletion SLAs — we’ll include KMS options and a scoped retention policy in the contract.

Proposed timeline (high level):
1) 8/11 — this recap + share attachments (today)
2) 8/12 — confirm technical Qs / provide sample payloads & dataset access (Payline)
3) 8/15 — engineering sync request + finalize API contract draft (Redwood)
4) 8/21 — kickoff: connectivity test, SDK install, streaming smoke tests
5) 8/21–9/18 — trial window (4 weeks)

Ownership (initial):
- Redwood owner: Ben Carter (sales) — commercial lead; Karthik Iyer (R&D) — technical owner; Marissa Cole (CS) — adoption & metrics
- Payline owner: Sophie Turner — product; procurement@payline.com — legal/contract

Immediate asks for Payline (please):
- Confirm the 4-week window and kickoff date (8/21)
- Provide a 5k labelled sample or representative anonymized set by EOD 8/12
- Confirm whether Payline will require an on-premises connector or a VPC-only trial environment (data residency)

Attachments included: poc-success-criteria.xlsx, proposed-api-contract-v1.pdf, redwood-sdk-integration-notes.md (short integration bullets).

If that looks good I’ll send a calendar invite for the kickoff + a link to a shared Drive with the integration checklists.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0199

---


From: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@payline.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@payline.com
Date: Sun, 12 Aug 2028 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC evaluation & implementation plan — next steps

Ben — thanks for the clear recap. We’re aligned on the objective and generally okay with the timeline. A couple of quick clarifications from our side before we commit: 

1) Streaming & SDK: is the streaming path using server-sent events or websocket? We need to know client footprint (browser vs backend) since our fraud checks are in a Java service. Also how does tool-calling work — are calls proxied through Redwood or executed by our AS-IS endpoints?

2) Data residency / PII: we cannot send full-card PANs — we’ll provide tokenized traces. We do need retention + deletion SLA language in the contract. Can you clarify what level of KMS integration you support for the private deployment?

3) Evaluation data: we can provide a 5k anonymized set by COB 8/12 but can you confirm the preferred schema? We currently have: event_id, timestamp, merchant_id, tokenized_card, amount_cents, outcome_label.

4) Commercial: procurement looped in; they asked for a short SOW template and your standard trial terms (+ any credit/discount for an early production commit).

If you can answer (1) and (2) here, we’ll share the dataset and confirm kickoff. We’d prefer the engineering sync to be week of 8/21 — morning PT on 8/22 if possible.

— Sophie
Sophie Turner
Head of Product, Payline Systems
sophie.turner@payline.com | +1 206-555-0123

---


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 12 Aug 2028 14:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC evaluation & implementation plan — technical notes for reply

Ben / Marissa — quick technical notes to include in the client reply (so Sophie has concrete answers):

- Streaming: we support both WebSocket and HTTP streaming (chunked + SSE). For a Java backend I recommend WebSocket (less client-side buffering, bi-directional for tool-calls). Payloads are JSON with an initial handshake that includes model id, api_key (or mTLS cert), and a session_id for KV cache affinity. We’ll share example payloads in the Drive doc (redwood-sdk-integration-notes.md).

- SDK routes: two primary APIs for this POC — /v1/stream (websocket) for low-latency lookups and /v1/batch/generate for batched re-ranking. We’ll provide a minimal Java client snippet that shows connection, heartbeat, and reconnection policy.

- Tool calling: by default our runtime proxies secure tool-calls to customer endpoints over TLS; we can also support a callback pattern where Redwood posts a signed webhook to your internal service. For Payline’s lookup flow, proxying through Redwood avoids exposing endpoints directly and simplifies auth — but if Payline prefers, we can use signed callbacks.

- Data residency / KMS: for Private deployment (VPC) we support customer-managed KMS keys (AWS KMS or Azure Key Vault). For hosted trials we can enable envelope encryption with customer KMS keys; deletion SLA is configurable and we usually commit to 30-day max retention for trial data unless otherwise agreed.

- Capacity / throttling: based on your 15 req/s target, we’ll start with a single GPU instance (g16x1 equivalent) and enable autoscale if throughput spikes. We’ll provide rate-limiting headers and onboarding guidance for throttling back off at client side.

Action items for reply: add the example payloads link, confirm we can accept tokenized PANs (fine), and propose the engineering sync on 8/22 9am PT (Ben to propose in calendar). I’ll prepare the Java client snippet and a short runbook for tool-calling choices.

— Karthik
Principal Engineer, Inference Integrations
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai

---


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@payline.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, procurement@payline.com
Date: Tue, 15 Aug 2028 16:23:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC evaluation & implementation plan — next steps

Sophie — thanks for the questions. Consolidating details below and confirming next steps. Long answer short: WebSocket for streaming is recommended for Java backends; tool-calling can be proxied or webhook-based (your choice); we support customer KMS and a 30-day deletion SLA for trials by default.

Answers to your numbered items:
1) Streaming & SDK — we recommend WebSocket for Payline’s Java service. The streaming endpoint will be /v1/stream (WS). Example handshake and payloads are in the Drive folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2b3C-payline-poc. We’ll provide a Java client snippet + reconnection policy prior to the kickoff.

2) Data residency / PII — tokenized PANs are fine. For the trial we propose envelope encryption with your KMS (we can accept AWS KMS or Azure Key Vault). Deletion SLA: 30 days for trial telemetry and logs; we’ll include explicit language in the contract draft.

3) Evaluation data — schema you shared works. Please send the 5k anonymized sample by EOD 8/12 (if that’s delayed, send a 500-sample placeholder so we can run smoke metrics).

4) Commercial / procurement — looping in procurement is good. I’ve attached a short SOW template and the proposed API contract (proposed-api-contract-v1.pdf). If Payline signs the trial DocuSign, we’ll provision the trial environment same day. DocuSign link: https://www.docusign.net/Member/PowerFormSigning.aspx?PowerFormId=abc123

Confirmed dates & owners:
- Kickoff: 2028-08-21 (engineering sync week of 8/21) — proposed time 2028-08-22 09:00 PT
- Trial window: 2028-08-21 through 2028-09-18 (4 weeks)
- Redwood owners: Ben Carter (commercial), Karthik Iyer (tech), Marissa Cole (CS/metrics)
- Payline owners: Sophie Turner (product), procurement@payline.com (commercial)

Next steps (we’ll take these immediately):
- Ben: send calendar invites for kickoff + engineering sync (today)
- Karthik: prepare Java client snippet, tool-calling runbook, and a small load profile estimate (target 15 req/s)
- Payline: share 5k anonymized sample (or 500-sample fallback) and confirm KMS provider & data residency choice

Attachments: poc-success-criteria.xlsx (success metrics & test plan), proposed-api-contract-v1.pdf (trial contract + SLAs), redwood-sdk-integration-notes.md (integration checklist & example payloads).

If that all looks right I’ll route the DocuSign and lock the kickoff invite. Appreciate the fast turnaround — excited to get this running.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0199

title: Request: Field Research Consortium — pro-bono compute credits & trial extension
preview:
From: Sophie Martin <sophie@mapaid.org>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Dr. Ahmed El‑Sayed <ahmed.elsayed@univ-nile.edu>, procurement@mapaid.org
Date: Fri, 10 Aug 2028 09:14:00 +0000
Subject: Pilot request — cross-site field research (compute credits + extension)

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the helpful demo last month. MapAid's Field Research Consortium is preparing a multi-site pilot in three North African communities focused on offline mapping and humanitarian needs classification. The work is time-sensitive — heavy batch embeddings and nightly retraining from mobile-collected telemetry — and we are already bumping into our trial limits.

Two asks:
- A two-month trial extension (we need sustained access through Oct 2028) so the field teams can complete the annotation sweep and evaluation.
- A one-time pro-bono credit allocation of $10,000 worth of compute to cover heavy retrain/embedding runs and evaluation reruns.

Attached: short pilot proposal (MapAid_proposal.pdf) and the IRB-style ethics letter from our university partner (ethics_letter_signed.pdf). Procurement is looped (procurement@mapaid.org) in case there's any paperwork.

This is a public-interest project; we will acknowledge Redwood in outreach materials and can provide a short case study if useful. Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps.

Appreciate any flexibility here — the teams are very metalimited in the field and the research has real time windows.

Best,

Sophie Martin
Program Director, MapAid Collective
sophie@mapaid.org
+44 7700 900111

title: Observability telemetry, residency & subprocessor gating — post-demo follow-up
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Zhu <elaine.zhu@finlytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Aug 2028 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Observability telemetry, residency & subprocessor gating — post-demo follow-up

Hi Elaine — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our demo and the items you flagged on the call, plus proposed next steps so we can move this to security + procurement review: 

Recap / commitments from Redwood:
- Telemetry: by default we emit aggregated observability metrics and a short-lived request log for debugging. We can disable request-log collection for any route or customer on request, and we can configure sampling.
- Data residency: we support region pinning (US-East, US-West, eu-west) in Hosted and Dedicated; Private deployment gives full on-prem/VPC control.
- Subprocessors: primary cloud provider (GCP/AWS), telemetry partner (third-party metrics provider), and optional marketplace partner integrations. We will share the full subprocessor roster and contract language.
- Retention: default debug logs 30 days; request traces can be reduced to 7/3/0 days per customer SLA. Embeddings/text outputs are under customer control and can be excluded from telemetry.

Attachments I’m sending with this thread: redwood-soc2-audit-summary.pdf, DPA-template-redwood.docx, redwood-telemetry-dataflow.png (network/flow diagram).

Asks for FinLytics so we can align exactly on gating: 
1) Can you confirm whether any PII will be sent to Redwood as part of inference requests (names, SSNs, account numbers, etc.)?
2) Desired logs retention window for request-level logs and the retention for observability metrics (options: 30/7/3/0 days)?
3) Residency requirement (do you require US-only / US-East specifically? Any contractual language)?
4) Any required subprocessors not listed that must be excluded or approved up front (e.g., marketplace.io or a telemetry vendor)?

Proposed next steps: 
- I’ll loop in our security lead (Neha) to provide SOC2 / subprocessor roster and answer controls questions.
- If OK, we’ll add our DPA template and a mutual action plan (MAP) with dates: security review (this week), DPA + contract redlines (next week), procurement signature target within 3 weeks.

Available times for a short follow-up with our security engineer: Mon 8/7 9–11am PT or Tue 8/8 1–3pm PT. Let me know what works and we’ll calendar.

Thanks — Ben
Benjamin Carter
Enterprise Lead, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

---
Attachments: [redwood-soc2-audit-summary.pdf] [DPA-template-redwood.docx] [redwood-telemetry-dataflow.png]

From: Elaine Zhu <elaine.zhu@finlytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: FinLytics Security <security@finlytics.com>, Tom Rivera <tom.rivera@marketplace.io>
Date: Mon, 6 Aug 2028 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Observability telemetry, residency & subprocessor gating — post-demo follow-up

Ben — appreciate the clear summary. A few answers and clarifying asks from our side: 

Answers:
- PII: yes — our inference payloads may include customer names and account IDs; we will not send direct payment card data but identifiers are in scope.
- Residency: we require US-only processing for this product line; US-East is preferred due to latency.
- Retention: procurement/security prefer 7 days for request-level logs, and metrics can stay for 90 days if aggregated.

Clarifying questions / asks:
- Can telemetry (request logs) be fully disabled per route/customer? We have a small set of routes that process high-value PII where we would want no request logs retained.
- Marketplace flows: when our users enable the marketplace-driven connectors, does any request/response payload flow to marketplace.io or third parties? Tom, can you confirm marketplace behavior?
- Subprocessors: please share full list and whether subprocessors have access to raw request payloads.

I’ll drop our lightweight security questionnaire into the thread (attached) — it’s to help speed internal review. We’re aiming to complete security review by 8/14 if possible.

Thanks — Elaine
Elaine Zhu
Head of Product, FinLytics
elaine.zhu@finlytics.com | +1 212-555-0199

---
Attachments: [FinLytics-security-quick-qna.pdf]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Zhu <elaine.zhu@finlytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Aug 2028 13:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Observability telemetry, residency & subprocessor gating — post-demo follow-up

Elaine — great, thanks for the answers and the quick QnA. Inline on your asks: 

- Disable telemetry per route/customer: yes, we support disabling request-log collection at the customer routing layer. For Hosted customers this is a config toggle; for Dedicated/Private we can enforce via policy. We will document the steps and include it in the MAP.

- Marketplace flows: by default, marketplace integrations only receive metadata (integration status, non-sensitive event hooks). We do not forward raw inference request payloads to marketplace partners unless a customer explicitly enables a connector that requires forwarding. We’ll confirm the exact behavior for marketplace.io and produce a short flow diagram (attached).

- Subprocessors & raw access: our telemetry partner receives aggregated metrics and sampled metrics; they do NOT receive raw request payloads by default. Raw request logs are stored in our internal debug store (encrypted) and are accessible only to a small set of Redwood engineers under an audited workflow; access can be disabled entirely for your account.

Retention and residency options: 
- Request logs: default 30d, configurable to 7/3/0 days. We can set 7 days for FinLytics during onboarding.
- Metrics: we can keep aggregated metrics for 90 days.
- Residency: we can pin processing to US-East in Hosted or Dedicated; Private deployment gives full control.

Next actions I’m taking now: 
1) Neha will add our subprocessor roster and confirm contractual language we typically use for DPA/subprocessor gating.
2) I attached the MAP template and proposed dates (security review by 8/14, DPA redlines 8/18, procurement sign 8/24).
3) Please pick a slot for the security demo with our engineer — I proposed Mon 8/7 or Tue 8/8 earlier.

Ben
Benjamin Carter
Enterprise Lead, Redwood Inference

Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Aug 2028 08:21:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Observability telemetry, residency & subprocessor gating — post-demo follow-up

Hi Elaine / team — Neha from Redwood Security. Thanks for the detailed notes. A few technical confirmations and what we’ll provide by end of day: 

Confirmations:
- Encryption: all data in transit (TLS1.2+) and at rest (AES-256) by default. Customer-managed KMS (AWS KMS / GCP CMEK) is available on Redwood Private and Dedicated.
- Access controls: raw request logs stored in our internal debug store are role-restricted and audited. We can disable storage entirely for your account.
- Subprocessor access: our telemetry partner receives only aggregated or sampled metrics; they do not receive full raw payloads. Our cloud provider (AWS/GCP) has physical access as standard infra subprocessor.

Deliverables (EOD today):
- Full subprocessor roster (redwood-subprocessor-roster.csv) with each vendor, purpose, and data access scope.
- SOC2 summary (redwood-soc2-audit-summary.pdf) — copies of the report are available under NDA; we can route the full report to procurement.
- Suggested DPA redlines and example clause language for subprocessor gating and data residency.

Contractual note: if you require explicit pre-approval of any new subprocessor, we can include a subprocessor-gating clause in the DPA and commit to 30 days notice before onboarding a new vendor.

I’ll also schedule a short technical call with one of our infra engineers to walk the team through the flow diagram. Proposed slots: Mon 8/7 10am PT, Tue 8/8 2pm PT.

-neha
Neha Kapoor
Head of Security, Redwood Inference

- Metrics: we can keep aggregated metrics for 90 days.
- Residency: we can pin processing to US-East in Hosted or Dedicated; Private deployment gives full control.

Next actions I’m taking now: 
1) Neha will add our subprocessor roster and confirm contractual language we typically use for DPA/subprocessor gating.
2) I attached the MAP template and proposed dates (security review by 8/14, DPA redlines 8/18, procurement sign 8/24).
3) Please pick a slot for the security demo with our engineer — I proposed Mon 8/7 or Tue 8/8 earlier.

Ben
