title: Greenline Health - Order Form: rebate calculation & packaging smoothing (redline)
preview:
From: Sara Kline <sara.kline@greenlinehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@greenlinehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Greenline - Order Form redline: rebate formula & packaging smoothing

Ava —

Thanks for the walkthrough last week. Attached is Greenline's redline of the order form and Pricing Schedule Exhibit. The two items we need clarity on before procurement can sign are:

1) Rebate smoothing calculation: your current Exhibit B annualization treats quarterly usage as simple sum then prorates. We've redlined a rolling 12-month smoothing approach to avoid large Q4 true-ups. Please see highlighted formula in the Exhibit PDF.

2) Minimum annual floor vs. temporary ramp: we've proposed a temporary waiver of the annual minimum for Year 1 but with an earned-back claw if usage doesn't hit 60% of committed volumes by month 9.

We also flagged rounding/decimal rules (section 2.3) and a related ambiguity in the definition of Committed Usage.

Can you review the redline and confirm whether Redwood can accept the rolling-12 smoothing or propose commercial alternatives? We'd like to close this week.

Attachments: Greenline_Redline_OrderForm.docx; Pricing_Schedule_Exhibit_Redline.pdf

Thanks,
Sara
Sara Kline
Senior Procurement Manager
Greenline Health


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@greenlinehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Greenline - Order Form redline: rebate formula & packaging smoothing

Sara —

Appreciate the redline and the clear callouts. We'll get a short internal review and circle back with a clean counterproposal. Immediate notes:

- Ben (commercial) is reviewing the floor/waiver ask; initial thought is to offer a Year 1 ramp with a one-time rebate credit rather than changing the annual minimum language.
- Marissa (ops) will confirm billing acceptance for a rolling 12-month smoothing approach — specifically how we record usage and compute the KV cache of credits across quarters.
- I'll coordinate with Legal on the Committed Usage definition and rounding rules.

Propose a 30-minute call Wednesday morning to run through the examples (we'll bring a worked spreadsheet). Does 10:00am PT work for your team?

Putting Ben and Marissa on thread.

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@greenlinehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Jul 2028 09:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Greenline - Order Form redline: rebate formula & packaging smoothing

Sara / Ava —

Thanks, Sara. I read the redline and pulled together a short commercial alternative (attached). High level:

- We are open to a rolling 12-month smoothing approach operationally, provided the annual minimum floor remains intact as the commercial guarantee for Redwood capacity planning.
- To bridge the ramp concern, Redwood can offer a one-time, non-recurring rebate credit equal to 5% of estimated Year 1 committed spend if Greenline does not reach 60% of commited usage by month 9. The credit would be applied against the first invoice after month 12.
- We cannot accept an outright waiver of the minimum for Year 1 — the floor is tied to reserved capacity and forecasting.

Attached: proposed_rebate_calculation.xlsx (worked example comparing: current exhibit, Greenline redline, and Redwood counter).

If that approach is acceptable commercially, I'll hand to Legal for tightening language around timing, rounding, and the claw/earned-back mechanics.

Open to the Wed 10am PT call — happy to walk the numbers.

Best,
Ben Carter
VP, Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sara Kline <sara.kline@greenlinehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Jul 2028 15:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Greenline - Order Form redline: rebate formula & packaging smoothing

Ben / Ava / Sara —

Looping in legal comments inline below. I attached a marked-up clause-level redline of Exhibit B (Pricing Schedule) and a short summary of recommended language. Key legal points:

1) Definition clarity: we need Committed Usage defined as \"billable inference tokens consumed under the Order Form\" and specify treatment of development/POC usage (excluded unless explicitly converted). This prevents disputes on calls counted for smoothing.

2) Rounding & annualization (Section 2.3): accept \"rolling 12-month smoothing\" operationally, but require a cap on smoothing adjustment of +/- 2.5% per quarter to prevent back-end volatility. Suggested language: \"Smoothing adjustments shall not exceed two and one-half percent (2.5%) of the quarterly invoice amount when compared to the prior quarter.\"

3) Floor and one-time credit: accept Ben's commercial proposal conditionally. Contract language should state: (a) the annual minimum remains binding; (b) if usage < 60% of Committed Usage at the end of month 9, Redwood may, at its election, grant a one-time credit equal to 5% of the committed Year 1 fees applied to invoice after month 12; (c) credit is non-recurring and does not modify the annual minimum. Avoid phrasing that creates an implied waiver.

4) Clawback / Earn-back mechanics: require a simple reconciliation in month 12 with the following: computed usage vs. Committed Usage, credit applied, and a final true-up (positive or negative) limited to +/- the amount of the credit. This keeps Redwood exposure bounded.

5) Audit and evidence: require Greenline to provide monthly usage reports and reasonable supporting evidence; Redwood reserves 30 days to review before applying credits.

I will prepare a clean counter-exhibit reflecting the above and a short memo for the call. For the call script: we should present the worked example from Ben's spreadsheet and show the worst-case Redwood exposure under the credit.

Thanks,
Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts
Redwood Inference

Thanks for the quick review and the constructive counter. The rolling 12-month smoothing with the limited +/-2.5% quarterly cap looks workable to our finance team, and we prefer Redwood's proposed one-time credit to an outright waiver — that should be easier for our CFO to accept. Two asks before the call:

1) Can Redwood send the clean counter-exhibit with Ben/Laura's language tracked? We want to ensure the Committed Usage definition excludes POC/dev usage as you propose.
2) Provide the worked example in the form of a single PDF showing three scenarios (best/expected/worst) for Year 1 net cash flow impact so our FP&A can confirm.

We can do Wed 10am PT; please send the clean docs beforehand. Also we'll need a DocuSign-ready order form if we get alignment on the call.

title: Re: Order Form / MSA — representations, warranty & indemnity sequencing
preview:
From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Becker <markus.becker@optek.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 09:15 -0700
Subject: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Alex — thanks again for the walkthrough this morning. Procurement pushed back on two items the legal team flagged: (1) the sequencing where customer representations are a condition to indemnity and (2) the evidence/operations handoff for third-party IP claims. Attached is our current redline (OPK-4321_OrderForm_v3_redlines.pdf).

Quick asks: can Redwood sign up to the sequencing language that prevents automatic denial of indemnity when representations are later found incorrect, provided customer cooperates and remedies within 30 days? And separately, we need clarity on who will own evidence collection (engineer contact) vs. litigation counsel — OpTek prefers a named liaison model in the MSA.

We have an internal deadline to present a revised MSA/Order Form to finance on Thurs EOD. Happy to jump on a 30-min call today.

Thanks,
Laura Chen
Head of Procurement — OpTek Systems


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 10:02 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Laura — appreciate the redline. High-level response before we digest the PDF:

- Sequencing: Redwood is open to narrowing the sequencing so that a customer representation does not automatically void indemnity if (a) the customer cures or (b) Redwood had no reasonable notice and relied in good faith. We would want to preserve our right to decline indemnity where material misrepresentation was knowing or fraudulent.
- Evidence/liaison: we can name an operations liaison for evidence triage (SRE/Eng) and still reserve legal control for settlement decisions. Typical split: Redwood ops collects logs + model snapshots; Redwood counsel handles engagement with claimant counsel.

Kimberly / Ben — can you review Laura's redline and propose markups on the sequencing clause and the evidence-ownership matrix? If possible, I want a single-file redline back to OpTek by 5pm today so procurement can brief finance.

Alex Martinez
Head of GTM Partnerships — Redwood Inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 13:45 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Alex — reviewed Laura's attachment quickly and drafted two alternative inserts for sequencing: 

1) Cure-first sequencing (preferred by customer):
   \"Notwithstanding any other provision, Customer's breach of a representation shall not relieve Redwood of its indemnification obligations if Customer cures the misrepresentation within thirty (30) days after receipt of Redwood's written notice and cooperates in the defense. For avoidance of doubt, the foregoing does not apply where Customer's misrepresentation was made knowingly or with fraudulent intent.\"

2) Risk-allocation sequencing (preferred by Redwood):
   \"Redwood's obligation to indemnify shall be governed by the parties' respective obligations and the materiality of any representation. Where a representation is incorrect but the Customer promptly cooperates and remedies any issue which materially caused the claim, Redwood shall not be relieved of indemnity unless the misrepresentation was knowing or fraudulent or would reasonably be expected to have changed Redwood's decision to enter the Agreement.\"

On evidence ownership: I populated an \"evidence-ownership-matrix.xlsx\" that maps data types (request logs, session recordings, model artifacts, user prompts) to the owning party and retention windows. Main pushback: OpTek requested raw prompt logs be handed over on demand. We should push for redacted excerpts and a mutual evidence request process routed through legal (to avoid PII/PHI exposure).

I'll produce a clean redline and a short explainer paragraph for the procurement team.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel — Redwood Inference


From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Becker <markus.becker@optek.com>, Ellen Shaw <ellen.shaw@freemanfisherlaw.com>
Date: 2028-07-30 08:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Thanks for the quick read-through, Kimberly. OpTek's outside counsel (Ellen) looped in to confirm they can accept the cure-first approach but want a slightly longer cure window (45 days) and a clause that preserves forensic preservation obligations during the cure period.

On prompts: procurement accepts redaction but says litigation may need full prompts for forensic triage. They ask Redwood to confirm an operational SLA for producing evidence (48–72 hours after legal request) and a secure transfer mechanism (SFTP or encrypted link).

Can Redwood confirm whether a 72-hour SLA for curated evidence (logs + model snapshot) is feasible? If not, please propose an alternative with interim milestones.

Laura


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-31 16:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Laura / Alex — Ben here on Ops/Support. 72 hours for curated evidence is aggressive but achievable for prioritized incidents with a playbook. Proposed operational commitments: 

- T0: Legal request submitted via secure ticket (Doc: related_links)
- T+24h: Acknowledgement and identification of data owners; preliminary artifact export (logs index + retention manifest)
- T+72h: Delivery of curated evidence bundle (redacted prompts, request logs, relevant model snapshot). For production incidents requiring deeper retrieval, provide best-effort extended timeline and weekly updates.

We will not deliver raw customer prompt streams unless compelled by court order; instead, we will provide verified extracts and a notarized evidence manifest where required. We'll add this as language in the redline and include the evidence matrix. 

Ben Carter
Director, Customer Reliability — Redwood Inference
Attachment: evidence-ownership-matrix.xlsx

Head of GTM Partnerships — Redwood Inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Alex — reviewed Laura's attachment quickly and drafted two alternative inserts for sequencing: 

1) Cure-first sequencing (preferred by customer):
   \"Notwithstanding any other provision, Customer's breach of a representation shall not relieve Redwood of its indemnification obligations if Customer cures the misrepresentation within thirty (30) days after receipt of Redwood's written notice and cooperates in the defense. For avoidance of doubt, the foregoing does not apply where Customer's misrepresentation was made knowingly or with fraudulent intent.\"

2) Risk-allocation sequencing (preferred by Redwood):
   \"Redwood's obligation to indemnify shall be governed by the parties' respective obligations and the materiality of any representation. Where a representation is incorrect but the Customer promptly cooperates and remedies any issue which materially caused the claim, Redwood shall not be relieved of indemnity unless the misrepresentation was knowing or fraudulent or would reasonably be expected to have changed Redwood's decision to enter the Agreement.\"

On evidence ownership: I populated an \"evidence-ownership-matrix.xlsx\" that maps data types (request logs, session recordings, model artifacts, user prompts) to the owning party and retention windows. Main pushback: OpTek requested raw prompt logs be handed over on demand. We should push for redacted excerpts and a mutual evidence request process routed through legal (to avoid PII/PHI exposure).

title: Atlas Health — Hosted API residency, retention & deletion Q&A
preview:
From: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Atlas Health Security <security@atlashealth.com>
Date: Sun, 29 Jul 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Our security team is working through the hosted API questionnaire and flagged a few items we wanted to confirm so we can close the SaaS security review. I attached the Atlas security questionnaire and highlighted the residency/retention sections. Quick questions below:

1) For customer data stored in the hosted API, can you confirm which US regions the data may be resident in today (primary + replicated backups)?
2) Is there an option to prevent cross-region replication for a tenant (i.e., region-locking)?
3) What is the standard retention policy for raw request payloads, model inputs/outputs, and logs? Do you offer custom retention by contract?
4) When a data-erasure request is submitted, how long until the customer can receive a confirmation that data has been deleted? Is there an auditable proof?

Appreciate short answers for each; the security team prefers concise bullet responses they can paste into the questionnaire.

Claire
Security Program Manager
Atlas Health

Attachment: atlas_security_questionnaire.xlsx (spreadsheet with highlighted rows)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 29 Jul 2028 13:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Claire — thanks for the clear list and the highlighted worksheet. Answers below are intentionally concise so your security team can paste them into the questionnaire. I've attached a one-page retention summary and our erasure playbook for evidence.

1) Regions: Primary customer data for US tenants is typically hosted in us-west-2 (Oregon). We maintain optional multi-region replication to us-east-1 for failover; we do not replicate outside the U.S. for US tenants unless explicitly requested in contract.

2) Region-locking: Yes. Region-locking (no cross-region replication) is available as a tenant configuration for Dedicated and Hosted customers on request and must be enabled prior to tenant onboarding. We can include a region-lock addendum in the SOW.

3) Retention (default):
- Raw request payloads: retained for 30 days by default, used for debugging/attack detection; can be shortened to 0 days on request.
- Model inputs/outputs: ephemeral caching by default; persisted traces for quality monitoring retained 90 days unless customer opts out.
- Operational logs (access/auth logs): retained 365 days for audit and security purposes.
- We support contract-level retention customizations (e.g., 0/30/90/365 day options) for enterprise customers via the Dedicated/Private plans.

4) Erasure requests:
- Timeline: Standard hosted erasure workflow completes in 72 hours for indexed/deletable records. Full purge from backups and longer-term storage may take up to 30 days depending on replication and backup retention windows.
- Proof: We provide a deletion confirmation that includes a summary of what was removed and timestamps. For enterprise contracts we can provide an auditable report (log excerpts and a signed certificate of deletion).

Attachments: redwood_hosted_retention_summary.pdf, erasures_playbook_v1.2.pdf

If the security team needs a short sentence per question for the spreadsheet, tell me and I'll convert these into single-line responses.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Atlas Health Security <security@atlashealth.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Thanks Ben — that helps. Two follow-ups from the security team:

A) For the 30-day backup purge window: is there any guarantee that snapshots taken prior to a deletion request will no longer be restorable after the 30-day window? In other words, do you (a) truncate backup retention so deleted items are unrecoverable after 30 days, or (b) mark records as deleted but keep backups unchanged until snapshot expiry?

B) For the deletion confirmation / certificate: can you include the hash/ID of the tenant and the deletion job ID so we can correlate it to our change control? Also, what logs will you include in the attestation?

Appreciate if you can provide short answers (bullets).

Claire

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Jul 2028 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Claire — answers below (short bullets):

A) Backups:
- We do not alter historical snapshots retroactively. Snapshots taken before a deletion request remain as stored and expire per the snapshot lifecycle.
- Operational effect: Deleted records are removed from live/indexed stores within the 72-hour workflow; snapshots will become non-restorable for deleted logical records once the snapshot that still contains them expires according to snapshot TTL (typically 30 days in default config).
- For customers requiring stricter guarantees, we offer a "snapshot quarantine & rewrite" option in enterprise contracts: we proactively rewrite recent snapshots to strip deleted tenant data and then re-snapshot, reducing residual snapshot exposure to the shortest practical window. This is a contract add-on and incurs operational lead time.

B) Deletion confirmation / certificate:
- We include tenant ID, deletion job ID, timestamp (start/finish), and the classes of data removed (raw payloads, cached model outputs, index entries).
- Logs included: deletion job trace (service API calls), storage object IDs for primary stores, and a redacted summary of audit/auth logs that show the deletion request and execution. For enterprise customers we can provide S3 object manifest hashes where applicable.

If Atlas wants the one-line entries for the spreadsheet I can produce them now; otherwise Kimberly and I can prepare the attestation template for legal review.

—Ben

4) When a data-erasure request is submitted, how long until the customer can receive a confirmation that data has been deleted? Is there an auditable proof?

Appreciate short answers for each; the security team prefers concise bullet responses they can paste into the questionnaire.

Claire
Security Program Manager
Atlas Health

Attachment: atlas_security_questionnaire.xlsx (spreadsheet with highlighted rows)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 29 Jul 2028 13:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Claire — thanks for the clear list and the highlighted worksheet. Answers below are intentionally concise so your security team can paste them into the questionnaire. I've attached a one-page retention summary and our erasure playbook for evidence.

1) Regions: Primary customer data for US tenants is typically hosted in us-west-2 (Oregon). We maintain optional multi-region replication to us-east-1 for failover; we do not replicate outside the U.S. for US tenants unless explicitly requested in contract.

2) Region-locking: Yes. Region-locking (no cross-region replication) is available as a tenant configuration for Dedicated and Hosted customers on request and must be enabled prior to tenant onboarding. We can include a region-lock addendum in the SOW.

3) Retention (default):
- Raw request payloads: retained for 30 days by default, used for debugging/attack detection; can be shortened to 0 days on request.
- Model inputs/outputs: ephemeral caching by default; persisted traces for quality monitoring retained 90 days unless customer opts out.
- Operational logs (access/auth logs): retained 365 days for audit and security purposes.
- We support contract-level retention customizations (e.g., 0/30/90/365 day options) for enterprise customers via the Dedicated/Private plans.

4) Erasure requests:
- Timeline: Standard hosted erasure workflow completes in 72 hours for indexed/deletable records. Full purge from backups and longer-term storage may take up to 30 days depending on replication and backup retention windows.
- Proof: We provide a deletion confirmation that includes a summary of what was removed and timestamps. For enterprise contracts we can provide an auditable report (log excerpts and a signed certificate of deletion).

Attachments: redwood_hosted_retention_summary.pdf, erasures_playbook_v1.2.pdf

If the security team needs a short sentence per question for the spreadsheet, tell me and I'll convert these into single-line responses.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Atlas Health Security <security@atlashealth.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Thanks Ben — that helps. Two follow-ups from the security team:

A) For the 30-day backup purge window: is there any guarantee that snapshots taken prior to a deletion request will no longer be restorable after the 30-day window? In other words, do you (a) truncate backup retention so deleted items are unrecoverable after 30 days, or (b) mark records as deleted but keep backups unchanged until snapshot expiry?

B) For the deletion confirmation / certificate: can you include the hash/ID of the tenant and the deletion job ID so we can correlate it to our change control? Also, what logs will you include in the attestation?

Appreciate if you can provide short answers (bullets).

Claire

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Ng <claire@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Jul 2028 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Qs about data residency & deletion for Hosted API (attached)

Claire — answers below (short bullets):

A) Backups:
- We do not alter historical snapshots retroactively. Snapshots taken before a deletion request remain as stored and expire per the snapshot lifecycle.
- Operational effect: Deleted records are removed from live/indexed stores within the 72-hour workflow; snapshots will become non-restorable for deleted logical records once the snapshot that still contains them expires according to snapshot TTL (typically 30 days in default config).
- For customers requiring stricter guarantees, we offer a "snapshot quarantine & rewrite" option in enterprise contracts: we proactively rewrite recent snapshots to strip deleted tenant data and then re-snapshot, reducing residual snapshot exposure to the shortest practical window. This is a contract add-on and incurs operational lead time.

B) Deletion confirmation / certificate:
- We include tenant ID, deletion job ID, timestamp (start/finish), and the classes of data removed (raw payloads, cached model outputs, index entries).
- Logs included: deletion job trace (service API calls), storage object IDs for primary stores, and a redacted summary of audit/auth logs that show the deletion request and execution. For enterprise customers we can provide S3 object manifest hashes where applicable.

If Atlas wants the one-line entries for the spreadsheet I can produce them now; otherwise Kimberly and I can prepare the attestation template for legal review.

—Ben

- expected batching behavior (latency vs batch window tradeoffs)
- caching/prefix rules you recommend
- acceptance criteria for token cost variance during pilot

Attaching our internal procurement checklist for reference.

Would like to lock the MAP this week if possible — do you have a short checklist we can share with legal/finance?

Thanks,
Sarah
Sarah Novak
Sr PM, AI Integrations
PulseHealth

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Novak <sarah@pulsehealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP and cost verification asks for pilot

Hi Sarah —

Really appreciate the clarity. I attached a one-page MAP draft (redwood_map_draft_v1.pdf) and a short token sensitivity workbook (token-econ-sensitivity.xlsx) that shows our assumptions and a few scenario runs. High level:

- Pilot length: 6 weeks (2 weeks integration + 4 weeks live traffic).
- Acceptance milestone: agreed eval set (50 prompts) + <5% quality regression relative to baseline model on signatures.
- Cost verification: run two traffic scenarios (low burst and steady) and confirm measured tokens/$ vs model in workbook.

Quick notes on assumptions (we'll finalize these with your infra team):
- Tokens per request: we started with 350 avg tokens per user event (prompt 120 + response 230). Workbook has alternatives at 200/350/700.
- Batching window: default 10ms continuous batching; we include a 50ms policy for burst tolerance. Expect ~10–15% lower tokens/$ in 50ms but ~40–80ms added P95 latency.
- Caching: prefix caching for repeated prompts (session keys) estimated to reduce upstream tokens by 8–12% for clinical templates.

Can your engineering contact share a sample traffic PDF (simple histogram or list of peak windows)? That will let us run the workbook against realistic traffic and give procurement a numbers-backed guardrail.

Proposed next step: 30-min alignment call today or tomorrow to lock the MAP and hand the doc to your finance/legal for review.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
(Attachments: redwood_map_draft_v1.pdf (application/pdf), token-econ-sensitivity.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet))

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29T13:10:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: MAP -> ops clarifying questions before call

Quick internal note — copying the key ops/sales folks so we present a single playbook. Questions we should have answers to before the alignment call:

1) Traffic profile: what is the 95th percentile RPS during business hours? Need a histogram or 1-hour sample.
2) Latency SLOs: are they comfortable with P95 under 300ms for UI flows? If not, we should propose canary with higher-cost low-latency model.
3) Caching rules: do they plan to use deterministic templates (yes/no)? If yes, prefix caching makes sense — estimate cache hit-rate range (0%, 15%, 35%).
4) Batching policy: propose default continuous batching at 10ms; option to increase window to 50–100ms for background jobs.
5) Acceptance test for tokens/$: recommend defining a 3-day rolling window check during week 3 of pilot; if measured cost deviates >12% from model, trigger root-cause and optional rollback.

Action items for call: Priyom to review token sensitivity workbook and extract 3 scenarios (conservative/expected/aggressive). Neha to prepare a short note on SLO/cost tradeoffs for finance.

Kimberly Park
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference

From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Sarah Novak <sarah@pulsehealth.com>
Date: 2028-07-29T15:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP and cost verification asks for pilot (numbers)

Sarah / team —

Thanks for the prompt. I pulled the key numbers from the workbook and summarized below so procurement has concrete gating mechanics to approve the pilot:

Summary (baseline scenario):
- Avg tokens/request: 350 (prompt 120 / response 230)
- Expected tokens per 1k requests: 350k tokens
- Model throughput assumption: 120 tokens/sec per vCPU-equivalent slot (this is our conservative run)
- Expected tokens per dollar (hosted, inc. infra): ~12k tokens/$ at baseline quantization and batching assumptions

Sensitivity scenarios included in token-econ-sensitivity.xlsx:
- Conservative (200 tokens/request) -> ~16k tokens/$
- Base (350 tokens/request) -> ~12k tokens/$
- Aggressive (700 tokens/request) -> ~6k tokens/$

Caching + batching impacts (rule of thumb we used):
- 10% cache hit-rate -> ~9% reduction in billable tokens (depends on prefix similarity)
- Moving batching window 10ms -> 50ms can reduce effective tokens/$ by another ~8–12% but increases tail latency; recommend this only for non-UI traffic or background queues.

Proposed gating language for MAP (short, copy-ready):
- Cost verification milestone: By end of week 3, measured token consumption across a 72-hour window must fall within +/-12% of predicted tokens in the workbook for the agreed traffic profile.
- If drift >12%: joint investigation within 48 hours; remediation window 7 days; finance hold if unresolved.
- Pilot acceptance: quality + cost criteria met for 7 consecutive days in week 5.

I attached the workbook with scenario tabs named CONSERVATIVE/BASE/AGGRESSIVE. Ben — if you want, I can prefill the BASE tab with PulseHealth's sample histogram once they send it.

Priyom Das
Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
(Attachment referenced: token-econ-sensitivity.xlsx)

Hi Sarah —

- Pilot length: 6 weeks (2 weeks integration + 4 weeks live traffic).
- Acceptance milestone: agreed eval set (50 prompts) + <5% quality regression relative to baseline model on signatures.
- Cost verification: run two traffic scenarios (low burst and steady) and confirm measured tokens/$ vs model in workbook.

Quick notes on assumptions (we'll finalize these with your infra team):
- Tokens per request: we started with 350 avg tokens per user event (prompt 120 + response 230). Workbook has alternatives at 200/350/700.
- Batching window: default 10ms continuous batching; we include a 50ms policy for burst tolerance. Expect ~10–15% lower tokens/$ in 50ms but ~40–80ms added P95 latency.
- Caching: prefix caching for repeated prompts (session keys) estimated to reduce upstream tokens by 8–12% for clinical templates.

Can your engineering contact share a sample traffic PDF (simple histogram or list of peak windows)? That will let us run the workbook against realistic traffic and give procurement a numbers-backed guardrail.

Proposed next step: 30-min alignment call today or tomorrow to lock the MAP and hand the doc to your finance/legal for review.

Thanks,
Ben Carter

From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: MAP and cost verification asks for pilot (numbers)

Sarah / team —

Thanks for the prompt. I pulled the key numbers from the workbook and summarized below so procurement has concrete gating mechanics to approve the pilot:

Summary (baseline scenario):
- Avg tokens/request: 350 (prompt 120 / response 230)
- Expected tokens per 1k requests: 350k tokens
- Model throughput assumption: 120 tokens/sec per vCPU-equivalent slot (this is our conservative run)
- Expected tokens per dollar (hosted, inc. infra): ~12k tokens/$ at baseline quantization and batching assumptions

Sensitivity scenarios included in token-econ-sensitivity.xlsx:
- Conservative (200 tokens/request) -> ~16k tokens/$
- Base (350 tokens/request) -> ~12k tokens/$
- Aggressive (700 tokens/request) -> ~6k tokens/$

Caching + batching impacts (rule of thumb we used):
- 10% cache hit-rate -> ~9% reduction in billable tokens (depends on prefix similarity)
- Moving batching window 10ms -> 50ms can reduce effective tokens/$ by another ~8–12% but increases tail latency; recommend this only for non-UI traffic or background queues.

Proposed gating language for MAP (short, copy-ready):
- Cost verification milestone: By end of week 3, measured token consumption across a 72-hour window must fall within +/-12% of predicted tokens in the workbook for the agreed traffic profile.
- If drift >12%: joint investigation within 48 hours; remediation window 7 days; finance hold if unresolved.
- Pilot acceptance: quality + cost criteria met for 7 consecutive days in week 5.

I attached the workbook with scenario tabs named CONSERVATIVE/BASE/AGGRESSIVE. Ben — if you want, I can prefill the BASE tab with PulseHealth's sample histogram once they send it.

Priyom Das
Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Visibility delivery patterns for private deployment - SIEM and trace handoff
preview:
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.p@northridgelabs.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 29 Jul 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Visibility delivery patterns for private deployment - SIEM and trace handoff

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for the call this morning. As promised, I wanted to capture a concise delivery approach for observability from a Redwood Private deployment into Northridge's SIEM/tracing collection so you can run it by your infra/security teams. High-level goals:

- No direct store of customer data in our hosted plane; all telemetry exported from your VPC to your collection endpoints.
- Support multiple export modes (syslog/TLS, HTTPS/HEC, and S3 transfer with signed objects) so you can pick what works with existing Splunk/Elastic pipelines.
- Traces emitted via OTLP over gRPC or HTTP(s) with sample hook endpoints and stable authenticated egress.

Attached: a quick sketch of the recommended egress network pattern (private-obsv-delivery-sketch.png) and a template of the SIEM requirements doc to annotate (northridge-siem-requirements.pdf).

Questions we need answered to finalize a runbook:
1) Preferred ingest protocol for logs (syslog-tls vs Splunk HEC over HTTPS)?
2) FQDNs/IP ranges you can whitelist for egress, or will you accept an IP-range + mTLS?
3) Retention/archival evidence you need from us (SOC artifacts, sample exported events, signed checksums).

Next steps I propose: Ben can triage the syslog/HEC connector options; Marissa will draft a minimal evidence bundle for your audit team. If you confirm preferred protocol and whitelisting constraints, we’ll produce an implementation checklist and a small test harness.

Best,

Kimberly Park
Principal Solutions PM, Redwood Inference
---
Attachment: private-obsv-delivery-sketch.png (image/png)
Attachment: northridge-siem-requirements.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Samir Patel <samir.p@northridgelabs.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Cc: Northridge Labs Security Team <security@northridgelabs.com>
Date: Mon, 29 Jul 2028 10:01:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Visibility delivery patterns for private deployment - SIEM and trace handoff

Kimberly —

Thanks for the summary and the diagram. A few clarifications from our end:

- Our default is Splunk HEC over HTTPS (token + TLS) for structured JSON logs; we still have a legacy syslog pipeline but would prefer HEC for new integrations.
- We require the egress host(s) to be resolvable FQDNs and allowlisting at the perimeter; static IP ranges are possible but only for peered VPCs. Can you provide FQDNs and an ASN/IP range we can add to the allowlist?
- For traces, we use OTLP/gRPC to a collector (internal) on 443. We need a sample span schema and a short-lived test token so we can validate sampling and tag mappings.

On the compliance side, our auditors ask for: a) sample exported events for a 24-hour window with hashed PII removed; b) signed evidence that the export pipeline preserves event order and timestamps within acceptable skew (<=2s). Can Redwood produce a reproducible evidence bundle that includes checksums?

We'll loop in our SRE for scheduling a network test. Proposed window: Wednesday 2028-08-02 09:00 PT.

Samir
Director, Cloud Platform — Northridge Labs


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.p@northridgelabs.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 29 Jul 2028 12:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Visibility delivery patterns for private deployment - SIEM and trace handoff

Samir —

Appreciate the clarity. A few concrete replies and what we'll deliver for the Wednesday test: 

1) Protocols: Redwood Private can emit to Splunk HEC (HTTPS + Bearer token) and to syslog over TLS (RFC 5425). For HEC we send batched JSON payloads with sequence ids per batch to help you verify ordering. For syslog-TLS we can tag with a persistent instance id and timestamp microseconds.

2) FQDN/IPs: For private deployments we can either route to your collector FQDNs directly from within your VPC (preferred) or we can NAT through a short list of egress IPs we provide. If you want direct FQDN egress we need the exact hostnames so we can validate TLS SNI. Ive attached a small diagram of the egress pattern and an example tracehook config (tracehook-sample-config.yaml).

3) Evidence bundle: Marissa will assemble a signed bundle (zip) that contains: sample export logs (24h window, PII-masked), SHA256 checksums, and a short script to replay the message sequence for verification. The bundle will be time-stamped and signed with our code-signing key. We can also provide a per-test signed token to demonstrate integrity.

For the test window on Wed 2028-08-02 09:00 PT we propose the following quick plan: 
- 09:00: network connectivity check (whitelist verification)
- 09:15: sample HEC ingestion (10k events) + order verification
- 09:30: OTLP trace push (10k spans) + sampling check
- 09:45: evidence bundle handoff + checksum verification

If that works for you Ill create a short runbook and a Confluence page and share the links.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: tracehook-sample-config.yaml (text/yaml)


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.p@northridgelabs.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 29 Jul 2028 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Visibility delivery patterns for private deployment - SIEM and trace handoff

Samir —

All agreed. Ive created a provisional Confluence runbook and added the proposed test steps. Link below. Marissa will drop the evidence bundle by EOD Tuesday so your auditors can pre-check. Ben will send the FQDN/IP options early Tuesday so you can push to your firewall team.

Action items (owners):
- Northridge: confirm allowed FQDNs or request egress IPs (Samir/SRE)
- Redwood / Ben: publish exact egress FQDNs and optional IP-range (Ben)
- Redwood / Marissa: produce signed evidence bundle and replay script (Marissa)
- Both: run network & ingestion test on 2028-08-02 09:00 PT

Thanks, and well be standing by for the firewall details.

Kimberly Park
Principal Solutions PM, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference | kimberly@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0198

title: Re: Brightline demo — coordination & traffic probe
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@brightline.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-25T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Quick demo request — Redwood for recommendations

Hi Ben,

I signed up for Redwood earlier this week (trial org: brightline-2028) and would love to get a short walkthrough to understand fit for our product recommendations use case. We're currently evaluating a few inference platforms and want to see latency/cost tradeoffs and how caching/batching might help our workload.

High-level: Brightline runs a hybrid retrieval+ranker recommender. We're interested in embeddings for retrieval and a smaller reranker for personalization. Not looking to be prescriptive — just want to see how Redwood maps to this pattern.

Would you be able to do a 30-minute demo next week? Attendees would be me (product), Aisha (engineering lead), and possibly one ML engineer depending on timing.

Thanks —
Liam
Liam O'Connor
Head of Product, Brightline Commerce
liam.oconnor@brightline.com

Attachment: brightline_request_summary.pdf (pdf) — short one-pager attached from our side describing the current infra and goals.

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@brightline.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-25T12:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick demo request — Redwood for recommendations

Hi Liam — great to meet you and glad you signed up. Happy to run a focused 30-minute walkthrough. Before we lock a slot, a few quick questions so we tailor the demo to your workload:

1) Primary flow: do you want us to focus on embedding-based retrieval + rerank, or more on streaming/generation for personalization contexts?
2) Volume: what roughly is your peak QPS / concurrent requests for the recommender (or queries per minute)? If you have daily token estimates that helps too.
3) Payload: average request size in tokens (input + output) and whether you expect long context windows for personalization.
4) Timeline: are you looking for a short pilot (weeks) or production readiness (months)? Any fixed cutover dates?
5) Security: will pilot run on hosted Redwood or do you need Dedicated/Private for evaluation? Any compliance requirements (SOC 2, VPC, SSO) we should know now?

If you can shoot short answers, I’ll propose three 30-minute windows and attach a one-pager with what we usually cover.

Proposed slots (PDT):
- Tue Jul 30, 10:00–10:30
- Wed Jul 31, 14:00–14:30
- Thu Aug 1, 09:30–10:00

Or book any open time on my calendar: https://cal.com/ben/redwood-demo

Attachment: redwood_one_pager.pdf (pdf) — quick overview + suggested pre-demo checklist.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
SDR — Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

P.S. looping in Monica (CS) who can join if we get into pilot scoping.

From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@brightline.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Bennett <aisha@brightline.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T10:17:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick demo request — Redwood for recommendations

Thanks Ben — answers inline below. See attached diagram that shows our current infra.

> 1) Primary flow: do you want us to focus on embedding-based retrieval + rerank, or more on streaming/generation for personalization contexts?
We care most about retrieval+rerank (embeddings -> candidate retrieval -> small transformer reranker). No streaming/generation for this pilot.

> 2) Volume: what roughly is your peak QPS / concurrent requests for the recommender (or queries per minute)? If you have daily token estimates that helps too.
Currently: ~3M queries/day across global traffic. Peak sustained concurrency ~175 reqs/minute during promotional spikes. Average tokens per request (input + output) ~700 tokens.

> 3) Payload: average request size in tokens (input + output) and whether you expect long context windows for personalization.
Average 700 tokens; personalization context is ~1–2KB user history (usually summarized), so we may need 2–4k token windows for advanced experiments down the road.

> 4) Timeline: are you looking for a short pilot (weeks) or production readiness (months)? Any fixed cutover dates?
Pilot: 4–6 week pilot target, with production ramp in ~3–4 months if results look good. Goal is to validate latency and per-token cost pre-holiday.

> 5) Security: will pilot run on hosted Redwood or do you need Dedicated/Private for evaluation? Any compliance requirements (SOC 2, VPC, SSO) we should know now?
Pilot: hosted is okay for initial eval, but production requires SOC 2 + VPC peering and SAML/SSO. Retention policy: 30 days for PII-free telemetry.

Attaching: brightline_usecase_diagram.png (png) — high-level flow and data touchpoints.

Prefer the Wed Jul 31 14:00 slot if available. Attendees confirmed: me, Aisha, and our ML engineer (Sam).

Cheers,
Liam

title: FjordCommerce: funnel lockdown — sustained long-tail latency & 429s
preview:
From: Priya Shah <ops@fjordcommerce.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Amit Rao <amit@fjordcommerce.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T08:14:22-07:00
Subject: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Jordan —

We started seeing a surge of 429s and multi-second tail completions on our checkout personalization calls around 07:40 UTC. Traffic pattern looks normal on our edge but Redwood API replies are taking 5–12s on the 95–99th percentile and 2% of requests are 429-ing. This is impacting user checkout completion.

Attached: a short request sample and a handful of traces (we redacted PII). Can your team confirm whether this is a routing/concurrency cap or an internal queueing/backpressure event on your side?

We're getting pressure from ops and product — need a quick status and a mitigation path.

Thanks,
Priya
Priya Shah
Head of Platform Reliability, FjordCommerce
[attachment: diagnostics/fjord_request_sample.log (45KB)]

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Priya Shah <ops@fjordcommerce.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T08:36:05-07:00
Subject: Re: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Priya — thanks for the quick heads-up and the logs. We are pulling traces now. Preliminary notes: we see a noticeable in-flight accumulation on the model pool serving the "fc-personalize-v1" variant starting at 07:38 UTC. Token-level latency isn't elevated for short requests, but long requests (likely longer context / retries) are accumulating and pushing queueing into the p99 range.

Action items I am kicking off:
- Looping in David (runtime) for kernel/buffer saturation checks.
- Marissa to coordinate an immediate customer-safe mitigation (routing to dedicated nodes / transient concurrency bump).
- We will provide a putative mitigation within 30 mins and a root-cause readout within 3 hours.

Quick ask: Priya, can you confirm your request size distribution for the affected endpoints (avg tokens / % of >1k token requests)? That helps rule out customer-side prompt storms.

—Jordan
Jordan Blake
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
[attachment: redwood_trace_20280726.json (120KB)]

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T09:01:42-07:00
Subject: Re: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Jordan / Marissa — quick runtime debug notes based on the traces:
- Observed sustained queue depth increase on pool: hostset=dedicated-eu-1-prod? No — affected pool is "shared-gpu-a" in us-west.
- Concurrency caps hit on 4 nodes (cap at 64 inflight each); controllers are rejecting extra in-flight with soft 429s upstream.
- KV cache snapshot shows a growing number of stale prefix entries for fc-personalize-v1; cache churn appears limited but KV lookup latency spiked (~10ms -> ~120ms) during the window.
- OOMs are not present; no GPU kernel timeouts in the sampled traces.

Short-term mitigations I recommend now:
1) Bump concurrency cap for fc-personalize-v1 on shared-gpu-a to 96 (temp) and enable extra burst slots.
2) Route FjordCommerce keyspace to a warm dedicated slice if they can accept temporary dedicated routing (will reduce cross-tenant interference).
3) Purge stale KV prefixes for the variant and re-warm cache (we can push a cache rehydrate job).

I'll prepare the diagnostic tar (kv_snapshot_0726.tar.gz) and a concise ops playbook. If Jordan wants me on a call with Fjord ops in 15, I'm ready.

—David
David Kim
Senior Backend Engineer, Serving Runtime
[attachment: kv_snapshot_0726.tar.gz (2.1MB)]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Priya Shah <ops@fjordcommerce.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T09:13:59-07:00

Subject: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Jordan —

We started seeing a surge of 429s and multi-second tail completions on our checkout personalization calls around 07:40 UTC. Traffic pattern looks normal on our edge but Redwood API replies are taking 5–12s on the 95–99th percentile and 2% of requests are 429-ing. This is impacting user checkout completion.

Thanks,
Priya

Priya — thanks for the quick heads-up and the logs. We are pulling traces now. Preliminary notes: we see a noticeable in-flight accumulation on the model pool serving the "fc-personalize-v1" variant starting at 07:38 UTC. Token-level latency isn't elevated for short requests, but long requests (likely longer context / retries) are accumulating and pushing queueing into the p99 range.

Action items I am kicking off:
- Looping in David (runtime) for kernel/buffer saturation checks.
- Marissa to coordinate an immediate customer-safe mitigation (routing to dedicated nodes / transient concurrency bump).
- We will provide a putative mitigation within 30 mins and a root-cause readout within 3 hours.

Quick ask: Priya, can you confirm your request size distribution for the affected endpoints (avg tokens / % of >1k token requests)? That helps rule out customer-side prompt storms.

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T09:01:42-07:00
Subject: Re: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Jordan / Marissa — quick runtime debug notes based on the traces:
- Observed sustained queue depth increase on pool: hostset=dedicated-eu-1-prod? No — affected pool is "shared-gpu-a" in us-west.
- Concurrency caps hit on 4 nodes (cap at 64 inflight each); controllers are rejecting extra in-flight with soft 429s upstream.
- KV cache snapshot shows a growing number of stale prefix entries for fc-personalize-v1; cache churn appears limited but KV lookup latency spiked (~10ms -> ~120ms) during the window.
- OOMs are not present; no GPU kernel timeouts in the sampled traces.

Short-term mitigations I recommend now:
1) Bump concurrency cap for fc-personalize-v1 on shared-gpu-a to 96 (temp) and enable extra burst slots.
2) Route FjordCommerce keyspace to a warm dedicated slice if they can accept temporary dedicated routing (will reduce cross-tenant interference).
3) Purge stale KV prefixes for the variant and re-warm cache (we can push a cache rehydrate job).

I'll prepare the diagnostic tar (kv_snapshot_0726.tar.gz) and a concise ops playbook. If Jordan wants me on a call with Fjord ops in 15, I'm ready.

—David
David Kim
Senior Backend Engineer, Serving Runtime
[attachment: kv_snapshot_0726.tar.gz (2.1MB)]

title: Plan selection and upgrade window — questions on bundling and rollback
preview:
From: Clara Zhou <c.zhou@medifi.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-25 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Plan selection and upgrade window questions

Hi Ben / Lauren,

We're narrowing down between Redwood's Compact and Throughput tiers for our main inference workload. A couple quick questions before we lock in a path:

- If we pick Compact now and need to migrate to Throughput mid-contract, what's the upgrade window and typical disruption (canary, full cutover, downtime)?
- Conversely, if we start on Throughput and need to step down, how are credits / prorations handled?
- We also want to bundle the A/B evaluation add-on and the observability retention pack — are those combinable under a single tier discount, or do they remain additive?

We have a hard procurement approval deadline next Friday (8/2) and want to understand the rollback/escape hatch in case performance varies under load. Can you send a short note (or a single-page playbook) that we can attach to the procurement packet?

Thanks,
Clara

--
Clara Zhou
Head of ML Ops, Medifi
clara.zhou@medifi.io
+1 (415) 555-0193


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Clara Zhou <c.zhou@medifi.io>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-25 12:01:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Plan selection and upgrade window questions

Hi Clara — thanks for the clear list. Quick summary and an attached draft quote (redwood-quote-Medifi-v3.pdf) so you have numbers to work with. Highlights: 

1) Upgrade (Compact -> Throughput):
- Typical path: canary traffic split on Dedicated pools followed by full cutover. No planned downtime; KV cache warm-up does incur a short warm period where latency can spike for cold prefixes (we recommend a 24–48 hour monitoring window).
- Upgrade window: we can schedule the canary and full switch inside a 72-hour window. For procurement, we provide a 5-business-day SLA notice requirement for scheduling upgrades on Dedicated.

2) Downgrade (Throughput -> Compact):
- Downgrades are treated conservatively because of burst commitments. We offer stepdown credits pro-rated by days remaining, but larger committed discounts (reserved capacity) have minimum notice (30 days) and an associated stepdown fee. Metered seats and add-ons (A/B eval, observability) remain billable; we can convert observability retention to a shorter retention tier to preserve cost.

3) Bundling and discounts:
- Add-ons are additive by default. We can create a bundled commercial that folds A/B evaluation into the tier at a 10% bundle discount if the commitment is >= 12 months. Otherwise, add-ons remain line items.

4) Escape hatch / rollback playbook (short):
- Stage 1: Canary on 10–20% traffic for 24 hours, validate latency and token cost with Redwood Console dashboards.
- Stage 2: Expand to 60% for 24 hours; if regressions > 15% on P50/P95 we pause and rollback.
- Stage 3: Full switch and 48-hour monitoring window; if regressions persist we auto-fallback to previous model variant and open an incident.

I've attached a compact playbook (see PDF) you can include with procurement. Happy to turn that into a one-page PDF for your packet if helpful.

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: Clara Zhou <c.zhou@medifi.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-25 15:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Plan selection and upgrade window questions

On Jul 25, 2028, Ben Carter wrote:
> I've attached a compact playbook (see PDF) you can include with procurement.

Clara — quick commercial note to help procurement land: we can offer the following at signature for DEAL-9842 if you commit to a 12-month term and a modest reserved capacity minimum:

- 12% baseline discount on Throughput list price (or 8% on Compact).
- One-time migration credit of $12k to cover engineering migration/testing costs (applied after upgrade).
- Bundle discount: if you take A/B evaluation and 6-month observability retention, we’ll apply an additional 6% bundle credit on those add-ons.

I'll flag the migration credit in the quote (attached pricing-grid-2028Q3.csv). Ben — I'll need your OK to lock the credit before we finalize the quote.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Director, Commercial Operations, Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Clara Zhou <c.zhou@medifi.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26 08:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Plan selection and upgrade window questions — procurement steps

Clara — from a procurement/ops perspective, please note the following required items to avoid signature delays: 

1) PO wording: please reference Redwood Inference Inc., tax ID XX-XXXXXXX, and include the subscription term and the purchase order number.
2) SOW: we have a short SOW template; I attached a draft (medifi-sow-draft.docx). Please review and mark any changes.
3) DocuSign: we can push the final SOW through DocuSign once commercial terms are signed. Here’s the placeholder link to the draft: https://docusign.com/EXAMPLE/medifi-sow

On downgrades: the stepdown fees Lauren called out are standard for reserved pools. If you need a more flexible 'trial' path we can suggest a 90-day pilot on Throughput (metered) with breakpoints to convert to reserved. That avoids large stepdown fees if you decide to step back to Compact.

Kimberly Park
Senior Contracts Manager, Redwood Inference
kimberly_park@redwood.com


From: Clara Zhou <c.zhou@medifi.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-26 10:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Plan selection and upgrade window questions — confirming next steps

Thanks — this is exactly what procurement needed. A couple quick follow-ups: 

- We like the idea of a 90-day metered pilot on Throughput to validate tail latency under our real traffic. Can you include pilot terms in the revised quote and mark the migration credit as contingent on moving to reserved within 6 months?
- Can we get the one-page playbook as a PDF (good to include in our packet)?
- For scheduling: can we do a short 30-minute call to sign off on the SOW and pilot on Wed 8/1 at 10:00am PT?

If you can send a revised quote with the pilot options and the updated SOW link, procurement can route for approval next week.

Best,
Clara
--
Clara Zhou
Head of ML Ops, Medifi

title: Promo credit infusion request — overnight heatmap throughput sweep
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwoodinference.com>
To: Li Wei <li.wei@databarn.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Promo credit infusion request — overnight heatmap throughput sweep

Hi Li,

Thanks again for running the initial artifact against the sandbox. The DataBarn engineering team is ready to run a focused overnight heatmap sweep to validate latency at 350–450 RPS across three regions (us-west/us-east/eu-west). They specifically want to exercise prefix caching and slightly longer context windows to stress KV cache warmup.

Can Redwood provision a short, promotional credit infusion to support a 10-hour overnight window (target token budget ~1.8M tokens) tomorrow night? Reasonable ask is a one-time promotional top-up of 12k test credits (we typically convert to token units in ops).

I've attached the POC load plan and a traffic heatmap slice that shows the target windows. If you need a PO or signature, happy to loop in contracts — I can also provide a short mutual action plan.

Next steps we want to hit:
- Confirm credit top-up qty and ETA for provisioning (we're aiming to start at 22:00 PST)
- Any rate/throughput caps to be aware of so DataBarn can tune their client side
- Contact for emergency throttling during the run (hours and phone)

If you can't approve full amount, a partial uplift (~6k credits) would still unblock the core validation.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — this is a pivotal check for the 1.0 readiness signal.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Sr. Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
camila@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0136

Attachments: POC-load-plan.xlsx, night-sweep-heatmap.png

From: Li Wei <li.wei@databarn.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 09:43:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Promo credit infusion request — overnight heatmap throughput sweep

Camila —

Thanks for the detail and the plan. We can do the heatmap sweep tomorrow but need the promotional credit confirmation before EOD. A couple quick questions:

1) Is the 12k credits equivalent to ~1.8M tokens per your accounting? We want to ensure we don't under-allocate.
2) Is there any expected spike >450 RPS during ramp? Our client will keep to the profile you attached but we'll need to know if you have enforced per-minute caps.
3) Can Redwood set a safety throttle that will automatically drop to a fallback model if latency crosses the threshold? If so, we just need the contact for escalation.

If you can get an internal sign-off and a quick contracts go-ahead (PDF), we can confirm and schedule. Otherwise, a provisional 6k credit allotment will let us validate baseline latency and warm caches.

—Li
Li Wei
Platform Lead, DataBarn Inc.
li.wei@databarn.com

Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 10:05:00 -0700

Folks —

DataBarn asked for a 12k promotional top-up for an overnight heatmap sweep tomorrow night (target start 22:00 PST). They said 6k would be minimally acceptable.

Quick ask: can we provision a provisional 6k now and approve the remaining 6k pending contracts e-signature? If Finance needs a code path, we can flag as a promo credit and reconcile in the CRO monthly report.

Notes:
- This run will exercise longer contexts + prefix cache warmup in three regions (their load plan attached).
- We should consider setting a fallback to our smaller quantized variant if tail latency crosses 700ms (Li requested automatic fallback).

Marcus — do you foresee any capacity constraints on our side for a 10-hour 350–450 RPS run?
Priya — can you route a simple promo authorization PDF to contracts (contracts@databarn.com) and copy me?
Hannah — can you pre-set a throttling contact (on-call) and share the number?

Thanks — Camila

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwoodinference.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 12:21:45 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Promo credit infusion request — overnight heatmap throughput sweep

Camila — quick status:

title: SproutLab accelerator credit consolidation + conditional discount
preview:
From: Elena Torres <elena@sproutlab.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-23T08:07:00Z
Subject: Request: accelerator credit top-up + pricing path

Hi Ben,

Quick note from SproutLab — we run a cohort accelerator and are hitting the end of the free credits pool faster than anticipated. We have an active pilot (cohort B) using Redwood for model serving; usage spiked during demo week and the cohort is asking for a short-term top-up while we finalize commit/contract details.

What we're asking: a one-time promotional credit (top-up) of $20k to cover the next 6 weeks, plus a short-term promotional discount (20%) on billed usage for the first two months after credits run out while we finalize a longer-term commitment. We'd also like to understand the conversion path to a Reserved/Dedicated package (what's required to convert credits into committed dedicated capacity) — cost, notice period, and any conversion credits.

I've attached a usage snapshot for the cohort for the last 30 days. Happy to jump on a quick call if that's easier. We're under time pressure to confirm the cohort's environment by end of next week.

Thanks,
Elena Torres
Head of Platform | SproutLab, Inc.
elena@sproutlab.io

Attachment: SproutLab_usage_snapshot.csv (CSV)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elena Torres <elena@sproutlab.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-23T10:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Request: accelerator credit top-up + pricing path

Elena —

Thanks for the context and for the usage file, that helps. Short answers up-front: we can provision a temporary promotional credit, and we can offer a time-limited discount tied to a committed monthly spend. That said, approvals depend on a couple of gating items (finance sign-off, SOW + DocuSign, and a clear conversion trigger to Dedicated if you want predictable latency/SLA after the promo).

Proposal (draft):
- One-time credit: $20,000 applied immediately to the account to cover short-term overage.
- Promotional discount: 20% off billed usage for months 1–2 post-credit, conditional on a conversion commitment within 90 days.
- Conversion path: 30-day notice + signed addendum; conversion credit equal to 25% of unused committed capacity applied towards Dedicated onboarding (applies only if you sign a 12-month committed SKU at >= $15k/month).

If that framework works, I'll loop in Marissa for promo exception approval and Laura in finance for invoicing/SOW cadence. Can you confirm the cohort's expected monthly burn after the top-up (ballpark) and whether SproutLab can commit to the 12-month option if we provide the conversion credit?

I'll attach a short SOW draft and a DocuSign link once we have internal approval.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

Attachment: (will attach SproutLab_SOW_v1.pdf after approvals)

On Wed, Jul 23, 2028 at 08:07 Elena Torres <elena@sproutlab.io> wrote:
> (original message above)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-23T12:40:00Z
Subject: Re: Request: accelerator credit top-up + pricing path

Ben — looping in finance. Per our accelerator promo exception guidelines I can approve a limited one-time credit but want to lock terms so we don't create open-ended precedent. Things to flag for Elena when you reply:

1) Promo mechanics: credit applied to current invoice cycle, non-refundable, expires if not consumed within 90 days.
2) Discount window: limited to first two billing cycles post-credit and explicitly conditioned on a signed conversion addendum within 90 days (we should define the exact conversion SKU/committed monthly amount in the SOW).
3) Accounting constraints: finance wants the deal recorded in HubSpot with a custom line item for the promotional credit and a PO or signed SOW to pick up the conversion credit.

Recommendation: Offer $20k credit + 20% discount for 2 months if SproutLab signs a 12-month committed plan at $15k/mo or higher. If they cannot guarantee 12 months, propose a smaller committed term (6 months) with a lower conversion credit (15%).

Ben, please send the updated SOW with explicit committed SKUs. Laura — do you need any additional paperwork or approval flow for this size?

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Director, Growth Partnerships | Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com

On Wed, Jul 23, 2028 at 10:02 Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> (Ben's reply above)

From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-23T16:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Request: accelerator credit top-up + pricing path

Team — finance sign-off (conditional). We can approve the $20,000 promotional credit and the 20% promotional discount for two billing cycles under these conditions:

- Signed SOW + DocuSign covering the promo terms and the committed-conversion clause. Include exact SKUs and committed monthly amount (we need the price ladder so we can book revenue correctly).
- HubSpot deal record updated with the promo line item and the attached promo approval form completed by Growth (Marissa).
- Account to provide a PO or committed payment instrument (card or invoice terms) at time of conversion.

I've attached the internal promo_approval_form.pdf — please fill and attach to the HubSpot record. Also request that Ben add the conversion SKU to the SOW and route the DocuSign envelope to finance (cc laura@redwood.com) when sending to the customer so we can reconcile.

Once those items are present we will process the credit in billing and mark it as a promotional contra-revenue line; note this will affect recognition, so coordinate with accounting on start/end dates.

Laura Bennett
Head of Revenue Ops | Redwood Inference
laura@redwood.com

- Conversion offer: 25% conversion credit towards Dedicated onboarding if SproutLab signs a 12-month committed plan at $15k/month or higher within 90 days. If SproutLab prefers a 6-month commitment, conversion credit is 15% and the discount window remains 2 cycles.

Next steps:
1) I will send the SOW (SproutLab_SOW_v1.pdf) via DocuSign. Please review and sign; we will route a copy to finance. DocuSign link: https://docusign.net/envelope/1234abcd

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

title: Term & Auto‑Renew: Notice routing + legal signoff (Quasar Labs)
preview:
From: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kelly Nguyen <kelly@quasarlabs.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-07-19T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Term & Auto‑Renew: Notice routing + legal signoff (Quasar Labs)

Hi Kelly,

Thanks again for the call earlier — good to align on timelines. Attached is our clean SOW (Quasar_SOW_v3.docx) and an annotated redline showing the changes you requested on Term, Auto‑Renew, and Termination for Convenience (redlines-annotated.pdf).

Quick summary of the outstanding items we think need confirmation from your side:
- Notice window for non‑renewal: you mentioned 90 days; our standard is 30 days. Can you confirm whether 90 is a hard requirement or negotiable?
- Auto‑renew mechanic: currently set to 12‑month renewal terms. Do you want the ability to opt out for renewals via a procurement notice, or do you prefer contractually fixed renewals?
- Termination for convenience (TFC): you asked for mutual TFC. We can consider mutual TFC but would typically expect a ramp/transition assistance clause tied to committed spend.

If you confirm the 90‑day notice and mutual TFC, we’ll need to coordinate a short legal review with our counsel (cc’d Lauren) and Tech Ops for transition SLA commitments. Proposed next steps:
1) You confirm the notice window and whether mutual TFC must be unconditional.
2) We exchange a short redline (we’ve started that in the attached PDF).
3) If both sides accept, we’ll route to DocuSign and provide a 48‑hour turnaround for signatures.

If helpful, I can propose compromise language (60 days + enterprise carveout) — happy to draft.

Best,
Jonas Weber
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Quasar_SOW_v3.docx (docx)]
[Attachment: redlines-annotated.pdf (pdf)]

From: Kelly Nguyen <kelly@quasarlabs.com>

Cc: procurement@quasarlabs.com

Subject: Re: Term & Auto‑Renew: Notice routing + legal signoff (Quasar Labs)

Jonas — thank you.

Quasar’s position: we need a 90‑day non‑renewal notice for this engagement due to internal planning cycles, and we require mutual termination for convenience with no-cause termination available to both parties. Procurement is looped for formal review (procurement@quasarlabs.com).

A couple of clarifications:
- If mutual TFC is accepted, Quasar will expect 30 days of transition support (knowledge transfer + export of KV caches where applicable).
- Please confirm whether auto‑renew is a hard swap into a new 12‑month period or if we can include a one‑time opt‑out in year two.

We’re aiming to finalize commercial terms by end of next week. If Redwood can provide clean language that includes the 90‑day notice and the mutual TFC with the described transition support, procurement will proceed.

Kelly Nguyen
Head of Cloud Ops
Quasar Labs

Subject: Fwd: Term & Auto‑Renew: Notice routing + legal signoff (Quasar Labs)

Folks — forwarding Kelly’s response. Quasar insists on 90 days + mutual TFC with 30 days transition support. Deal details: QSR-2028-091, TCV ~ $420k first year.

Questions:
- Legal (Markus, Lauren) — can we accept mutual TFC as requested? If yes, what language do you want for transition assistance (add to SOW or MSA)?
- Commercial (Ben) — does 90 days materially affect our renewal mechanics or revenue recognition / billing cadence?

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-07-22T16:43:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Term & Auto‑Renew: Notice routing + legal signoff (Quasar Labs)

Jonas — thanks for the ping and context. Quick take from Commercial + legal alignment below.

1) Notice window: we typically do 30 days for self‑serve and standard customers; enterprise deals can accept longer notice windows if paired with firm commit or extended transition assistance. For Quasar (TCV $420k) I support the enterprise carveout approach.

Suggested customer‑facing compromise language (please copy into the order form/redline):
- "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon sixty (60) days prior written notice. Notwithstanding the foregoing, for Orders with a total committed spend equal to or greater than Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (USD $400,000) in the applicable Term, the non‑renewal notice period shall be ninety (90) days. Termination for convenience shall include a thirty (30) day transition assistance period during which Provider will provide reasonable offboarding support as set forth in the applicable SOW."

2) Auto‑renew mechanic: keep 12‑month renewal terms but add an express procurement opt‑out provision: client may opt out of a renewal one time in the second year by providing written notice within the applicable notice window. That protects us while giving them flexibility.

3) Transition assistance & SLAs: put transition assistance obligations in an SOW addendum (billable at discounted hourly rates up to N hours) and include a KV export/data export clause with timing. We do not want open‑ended obligations.

4) DocuSign & routing: once Jonas sends the redline with the language above, we should route to Lauren for final legal polish and then to DocuSign. Lauren — please confirm turnaround on polish (24 hours ok?). Markus — please confirm if the above TFC wording creates any indemnity or risk flags.

Next steps for Jonas: send Quasar the redline with the language in clause 1 and the opt‑out in clause 2; include the SOW addendum for transition assistance as attached. I’ll coordinate final approval and push for DocuSign routing.

—Ben Carter
Director, Commercial Operations
Redwood Inference
[Attachment referenced: tfc-proposal-redline.docx (docx)]
